methods_all.rules_or_formula: Average PE = (Sum of positive PE ratios / Number of firms with positive earnings) * Adjustment factor

methods_all.name: Aggregating Values
methods_all.description: Aggregate market value and net income/loss across all firms, including those with negative earnings, to compute a single price-earnings ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Aggregate PE = (Total Market Value / Total Net Income)

methods_all.name: Using Earnings Yield
methods_all.description: Calculate the inverse of the price-earnings ratio for all firms, including those losing money.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Earnings Yield = 1 / Price-Earnings Ratio

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Valuation
methods_all.description: Calculates intrinsic value of a company based on its future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: V = Ω / (k - g)

methods_all.name: EV-to-FCFF multiple
methods_all.description: Calculates enterprise value based on free cash flow to firm.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EV = FCF / (r - g)

methods_all.name: Relative Valuation
methods_all.description: Valuing a firm by comparing it to similar firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Calculate multiples for comparable firms and adjust based on differences in risk, growth, and cash flows.

methods_all.name: PEG ratio
methods_all.description: Adjusts the P/E ratio for expected earnings growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG = P/E / EPS Growth Rate

methods_all.name: Sector Regression
methods_all.description: Regression analysis to predict PE ratios based on risk and growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE = β0 + β1 * Risk + β2 * Growth

methods_all.name: Market Regression
methods_all.description: Regressing multiples against variables to predict values for individual companies.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Regress any multiple (PE, EV/EBITDA, PBV) against the variables that determine each multiple using all firms in the market sample.

methods_all.name: Relative Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimate the value of an asset by comparing it with similar assets.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Convert prices into multiples, standardize them based on earnings, book value, revenue or sector-specific variables, and compare these multiples across firms.

methods_all.name: cluster analysis
methods_all.description: statistical technique for finding similar firms

methods_all.name: Price-Earnings Ratio
methods_all.description: Ratio of market price per share to earnings per share.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE = Market Price Per Share / Earnings Per Share

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculation of PE ratio based on different measures of earnings per share.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE = Price / EPS (where EPS can be trailing, current, or forward)

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Uses diluted earnings per share to estimate PE ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Diluted EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Shares Outstanding (including options)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Model
methods_all.description: Used to derive PE ratio for stable and high-growth firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE = (1 + g) / (r - g), where g is growth rate, r is required return

methods_all.name: Two-Stage Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Calculates intrinsic value based on high growth and stable growth periods.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P = EPS0 * (1 + g)^n / (k_e,hg - g) + (EPS0 * (1 + g)^n * Payout_n * ROE_st / (k_e,st - g_n)) / (k_e,st - g_n)

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating the price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio based on expected growth rates and payout ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE = (Expected Growth Rate / (Cost of Equity - Expected Growth Rate)) * Payout Ratio

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the PE ratio based on expected growth rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE ratio = f(Expected Growth Rate)

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the impact of risk on PE ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: A firm with a higher cost of equity will trade at a lower multiple of earnings than a similar firm with a lower cost of equity.

methods_all.name: PE ratio vs. beta relationship
methods_all.description: Analyzing how PE ratios change with varying levels of risk (beta) and growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: As beta increases, PE ratio decreases; difference in PE ratios across growth classes is less at high betas and increases as beta decreases.

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate the price-earnings ratio for a stable growth or high-growth firm.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses fundamentals to determine PE ratios.

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates PE ratio based on economic fundamentals.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (0.5 × (1 + Expected Inflation))/((T-bond rate - Expected Inflation) - Dividend Yield)

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Predicting EP ratio based on T-bill and T-bond rates.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (EP ratio prediction formula not fully provided in text)

methods_all.name: Cross-sectional regression
methods_all.description: Regression analysis of PE ratios on interest rates and expected growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE ratio = β0 + β1 * Interest Rate + β2 * Expected Growth

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating the relationship between PE ratios and variables such as real growth, country risk, and interest rates.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE ratio = f(real growth, country risk, interest rate)

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Analysis
methods_all.description: Comparing actual PE ratios with analyst estimates of expected growth in earnings per share.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Regressing the PE ratio for the sector against the expected growth rate and the emerging market dummy yields: Firms with higher growth have significantly higher PE ratios than firms with lower expected growth. Emerging market telecom firms should trade at a much lower PE ratio than those in developed markets.

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Using regression analysis to predict PE ratios for firms.

methods_all.name: Price-Earnings Ratio Regression
methods_all.description: Regression analysis of price-earnings ratio based on growth rate, payout ratio, and beta.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE = a + bg + cπ − dβ

methods_all.name: Regression Methodology
methods_all.description: Compresses large amounts of data into one equation capturing the relationship between PE ratios and financial fundamentals.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE ratio = f(financial fundamentals)

methods_all.name: Market Regression
methods_all.description: Estimating PE ratio for a company using broader regressions.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Predicted PE = f(Growth rate, Beta, Payout ratio)

methods_all.name: PE ratio valuation
methods_all.description: Valuation method using P/E ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Expected PE = regression result; Actual PE < Expected indicates undervaluation.

methods_all.name: Normalized Earnings
methods_all.description: Adjusting earnings for inflation and averaging over time to smooth out cyclicality.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Average EPS across a cycle, adjust for inflation.

methods_all.name: PEG Ratio
methods_all.description: Ratio of PE ratio to expected growth rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG = PE / Growth; Lower PEG indicates undervaluation.

methods_all.name: PEG Ratio
methods_all.description: Price-earnings ratio divided by expected growth rate in earnings per share.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG = PE / Growth Rate

methods_all.name: PEG Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Uses consensus estimates of earnings per share growth over the next five years and current PEs.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG = PE / EPS Growth Rate

methods_all.name: PEG Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Uses analyst estimates to calculate PEG ratios for firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG = Price/Earnings ÷ Expected Growth Rate

methods_all.name: PEG ratio calculation
methods_all.description: Estimating the PEG ratio for a firm using its growth rate and payout ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG Ratio = (P/E) / g, where g is the expected growth rate.

methods_all.name: PEG Ratio Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing PEG ratios in relation to firm risk and payout ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: As beta increases, the PEG ratio decreases. For a given growth rate, firms with higher returns on equity should have a higher PEG ratio.

methods_all.name: PEG ratio calculation
methods_all.description: Estimates the PEG ratio for firms based on their fundamentals.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG = PE / growth rate

methods_all.name: Regression Approach
methods_all.description: Use regression analysis to control for differences in risk, growth, and payout ratios when comparing PEG ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Relate PEG ratios of firms being compared to measures of risk, growth potential, and the payout ratio.

methods_all.name: Regression Transformation
methods_all.description: Transform growth rate to natural log for linear relationship.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: ln(Expected Growth Rate)

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing relationship between PEG ratios and expected growth, beta, and payout ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG ratios vs ln(expected growth), beta, and payout ratio regression

methods_all.name: PEG Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates PEG ratio using expected growth rate and P/E ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PEG = P/E / Expected Growth Rate

methods_all.name: Relative PE Ratio
methods_all.description: Compare a company's PE ratio to its historical norms or to firms in different markets.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE_ratio_company / PE_ratio_market

methods_all.name: PE Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate future earnings per share and compare across firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Compare PE ratios of firms with similar risk, growth, and payout characteristics after year 5.

methods_all.name: Relative PE Ratios
methods_all.description: Adjust for market growth rate differences when comparing firm valuations.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Firms growing slower than the market will appear cheaper at higher market growth rates.

methods_all.name: Forward PE Ratios
methods_all.description: Estimating future earnings growth and valuing firms with negative earnings.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Calculate forward PE ratios by projecting future earnings before R&D expenses.

methods_all.name: Forward PE
methods_all.description: Estimate future earnings per share and divide by current stock price.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Forward PE = Current Stock Price / Expected EPS in Future Year

methods_all.name: Forward PE Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculate forward PE using forecasted EPS.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Forward PE = Stock Price / Forecasted EPS

methods_all.name: P/E before R&D
methods_all.description: Adjust P/E ratio by adding back R&D expenses to net loss.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Adjusted EPS = Reported Net Loss + R&D Expense; Adjusted P/E = Stock Price / Adjusted EPS

methods_all.name: Enterprise Value to EBITDA Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the total market value of a firm, net of cash, relative to its EBITDA.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EV/EBITDA = (Market Value of Equity + Debt - Cash) / EBITDA

methods_all.name: EV/EBITDA calculation
methods_all.description: Estimate the enterprise value to EBITDA multiple for a company.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Adjust numerator and denominator based on ownership percentage of subsidiaries.

methods_all.name: Enterprise Value to EBITDA Multiple Analysis
methods_all.description: Determining the enterprise value to EBITDA multiple using a free cash flow to the firm valuation model.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EV/EBITDA = (Free Cash Flow / Cost of Capital) + Terminal Value / (1 + Cost of Capital)^n - Reinvestment Requirements

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Method
methods_all.description: Determines the value to EBITDA based on a firm's cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Firms with lower costs of capital should trade at higher multiples of EBITDA.

methods_all.name: Expected Growth Method
methods_all.description: Evaluates the impact of expected growth on the value to EBITDA.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Firms with higher expected growth should trade at higher multiples of EBITDA.

methods_all.name: EV/EBITDA and Net Cap Ex Ratios
methods_all.description: Estimates firm value multiples for stable-growth or high-growth firms based on their fundamentals.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses EV/EBITDA multiple in capital-intensive firms with heavy infrastructure investments when depreciation methods vary widely across firms.

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Predicts value to EBITDA multiple for firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses regression analysis to predict the EBITDA multiple for a firm, comparing it against actual multiples to determine valuation status.

methods_all.name: valuation using earnings multiples
methods_all.description: determine firm value based on expected growth, risk, and cash flow potential
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P/E ratio = (Expected Growth / (Cost of Equity - Expected Growth)) * (1 + Tax Rate) * (1 - Reinvestment Rate)

methods_all.name: Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Estimates stock price based on future dividends discounted at the required rate of return.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P = D1 / (r - g)

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Using regression analysis to control for risk, growth, and payout differences across firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Specify how to use the regression output to identify under- or overvalued stocks based on deviations from expected PE ratios given firm characteristics.

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating beta and cost of equity using historical data.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Growth rate over the previous five years was used as expected growth rate, betas estimated from CRSP tape.

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Divide market price per share by book value of equity per share.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P/B = Market Price Per Share / Book Value Of Equity Per Share

methods_all.name: Adjusting Book Equity for Buybacks and Acquisitions
methods_all.description: To adjust for the differences in price-to-book ratios due to buybacks and acquisitions, take out goodwill from acquisitions and add back the market value of buybacks to book equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Adjusted Book Value = Book Equity - Goodwill + Market Value of Buybacks

methods_all.name: Price to Book Value
methods_all.description: Measures the ratio of a company's market price to its book value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = Market Price per Share / Book Value per Share

methods_all.name: Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Used to explore determinants of price–book value ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P0 = EPS1 * (Payout Ratio) / (ke - g)

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating intrinsic price-to-book ratio for firms in stable growth and high-growth scenarios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = (ROE * b) / (Ke - g), where ROE is return on equity, b is the payout ratio, Ke is cost of equity, and g is expected growth rate.

methods_all.name: Two-Stage Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Calculates equity value for high-growth firms.

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimates PBV ratio using free cash flows to equity in a two-stage model.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = (FCFE / Cost of Equity) * (1 + Growth Rate) / (Cost of Equity - Growth Rate)

methods_all.name: Expected growth rate calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates expected growth based on ROE and payout ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Expected growth rate = ROE × (1 – Potential payout ratio)

methods_all.name: Price–Book Value Ratio
methods_all.description: Calculates the ratio of a company's market price to its book value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price–Book Value Ratio = Market Price per Share / Book Value per Share

methods_all.name: ROE-based moat measurement
methods_all.description: Measures company strength through ROE.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Strength of a company's moat is indicated by the level and sustainability of its Return on Equity (ROE).

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Value Ratio Analysis
methods_all.description: Compare PBV ratios of firms within a sector and across markets.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = Market Price per Share / Book Value per Share

methods_all.name: Matrix Approach
methods_all.description: Plotting price-to-book value ratios against excess returns to identify misvaluations.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: None provided, but involves visual analysis of plotted data points.

methods_all.name: Regression Approach
methods_all.description: Regress price-to-book ratio against return on equity to predict undervalued or overvalued firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price-to-Book Ratio = β0 + β1 * Return on Equity

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Regressing price-to-book ratio against return on equity and risk.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price-to-Book Ratio = β0 + β1 * Return on Equity + β2 * Risk

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis for PBV Ratios
methods_all.description: Estimate price-to-book ratios using regression analysis with risk, growth, return on equity, and payout as independent variables.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = α + β1(Payout) - β2(Beta) + β3(EGR) + β4(ROE)

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Regressing price-to-book ratios against financial fundamentals.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = a + b1(Expected growth) + b2(Payout) - b3(Beta) + b4(ROE)

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Value Ratio Analysis
methods_all.description: Uses the price to book value ratio to determine if a stock is fairly valued.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Stock valuation = Price / Book Value

methods_all.name: forecast return on equity
methods_all.description: method to predict future performance of a firm's ROE.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: 1. Compute historical average ROE over past three or five years and use it as forecast if current ROE is volatile. 2. Adjust current ROE towards industry average considering competitive pressures.

methods_all.name: PBV/ROE Quadrant Movement
methods_all.description: A company moves from low PBV/low ROE to high PBV/high ROE as its performance improves.

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Value Ratio Strategy
methods_all.description: Screening for undervalued stocks based on low price-to-book value ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Select stocks with a book-to-price ratio (low PBV) to achieve excess returns.

methods_all.name: Price–Book Value Ratios
methods_all.description: Screening stocks with low price-to-book ratios for excess returns.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Select firms with a price-to-book value ratio below a certain threshold, typically less than two-thirds of book value.

methods_all.name: Value-to-Book Ratio
methods_all.description: Measures firm value relative to book value of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Market Value of Debt + Market Value of Equity / Book Value of Capital

methods_all.name: Value-to-Book Ratio Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing firm value multiples using a free cash flow to the firm valuation model.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: The value-to-book ratio is fundamentally determined by its return on capital (ROC). For high-growth firms, it's also influenced by cost of capital (k_c), growth rate, and reinvestment rate.

methods_all.name: DCF and relative valuation
methods_all.description: Measuring returns generated by a firm on its investments.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses ROC, ROIC, ROE for DCF; uses various measures for relative valuation.

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Examining the relationship between EV/IC and return on capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EV/IC = β0 + β1 * ROC

methods_all.name: D/C
methods_all.description: Debt-to-Capital ratio calculation.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Total debt/(Total debt + Market value of equity)

methods_all.name: ROC
methods_all.description: Return on Capital calculation.

methods_all.name: Tobin's Q
methods_all.description: Market value of assets divided by replacement cost.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Q = Market Value / Replacement Cost

methods_all.name: Tobin's Q
methods_all.description: Measures market value of a firm relative to replacement cost of its assets.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Q = Market Value of Firm / Replacement Cost of Assets

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Value Ratio
methods_all.description: Determines firm valuation based on its expected payout ratio, growth rate in earnings, and riskiness.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = (Expected Payout Ratio * Expected Growth Rate) / Riskiness + Return on Equity

methods_all.name: Value-to-Book Ratio
methods_all.description: Analogue to price-to-book for firm value, determined by return on capital, cost of capital, and reinvestment rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: V/B = (Return on Capital - Cost of Capital) / Reinvestment Rate

methods_all.name: Price-Book Value Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates PBV based on given financial metrics.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = (ROE * (1 - Dividend Payout Ratio) + Risk-Free Rate) / (Risk-Free Rate + Market Risk Premium - ROE * (1 - Dividend Payout Ratio))

methods_all.name: Price-Book Value Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates price-to-book value ratio using beta, payout ratio, ROE, and growth rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P/B = (ROE * (1 - payout ratio)) / (cost of equity - growth rate)

methods_all.name: Revenue Multiple
methods_all.description: Measures equity or business value relative to revenue.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price-to-Sales Ratio (PSR) = Market Value of Equity / Revenues; EV/Sales = Enterprise Value / Revenues

methods_all.name: Price-to-Sales Ratio
methods_all.description: Value of equity to revenues.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price-to-Sales = Equity Value / Revenues

methods_all.name: EV-to-Sales Ratio
methods_all.description: Multiple of the value of operating assets to revenues.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EV-to-Sales = Enterprise Value / Revenues

methods_all.name: Price-to-Sales Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculation based on stable growth dividend discount model.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P0 = EPS0(1 + g)(payout ratio) / (ke - g)

methods_all.name: Price-Sales Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the price-sales ratio based on profit margin, payout ratio, and growth rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PS = (Sales_0 * Profit Margin) / (k_e,hg - g) + Payout_n / (k_e,st - g_n)

methods_all.name: Price-to-Sales Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating PS ratio for high-growth firms using two-stage model.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Growth rate in first five years, cost of equity, growth rate after five years, payout ratios, net profit margin, risk-free rate are used to calculate the intrinsic price-to-sales ratio.

methods_all.name: Two-Stage Firm Valuation Model
methods_all.description: Uses beta, risk-free rate, and equity risk premium to estimate value-to-sales ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Dividing both sides by the revenue, you get: ATOM = EBIT(1 - t)/Sales; RIR = Reinvestment rate (RIR_n is for stable growth period); k_c = Cost of capital (hg: high growth and st: stable growth periods); g = Growth rate in operating income in high growth and stable growth periods.

methods_all.name: Two-Stage EV/Sales Equation
methods_all.description: Estimates intrinsic enterprise value using high growth and stable growth periods.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Plugging in the growth rate, reinvestment rate, operating margin, cost of capital for both stages.

methods_all.name: Enterprise Value Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates enterprise value using debt and cash balance.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (Debt Outstanding - Cash Balance)

methods_all.name: Revenue Multiples Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate value-to-sales ratios for stable-growth or high-growth firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value-to-Sales Ratio = (Net Margin * Sales/Book Value of Equity) + Operating Margin * Sales/Capital

methods_all.name: Price-Sales Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates price-sales ratio based on profit margins and sales/book value of equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: New Price-Sales Ratio = (Profit Margin / Sales/Book Value Ratio)

methods_all.name: Profit Margin and Turnover Ratio Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing the impact of pricing strategies on profit margins and turnover ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Divide after-tax operating income by sales to get the after-tax operating margin.

methods_all.name: Price Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates price based on sales/book value and book value of equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price = V/S × S/BV × BV

methods_all.name: Brand Name Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimating the value of a brand name using profit margins and price-sales ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: V/S_b - V/S_g

methods_all.name: Enterprise Value to Sales Ratio
methods_all.description: Estimates enterprise value based on sales.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EV/Sales = Enterprise Value / Total Sales

methods_all.name: Brand Name Valuation Method
methods_all.description: Assesses the impact of brand name on a company's valuation by comparing it with a less branded competitor.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses financial metrics like operating margin, return on capital, and EV/Sales to estimate value loss if the brand were removed.

methods_all.name: Brand Name Valuation
methods_all.description: Assesses the value of a brand name by attributing excess returns to it.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of Brand Name = Total Firm Value - (Firm Value without Excess Returns)

methods_all.name: Revenue Multiple Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing profit margins to identify overvalued or undervalued securities.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Firms with high revenue ratios and low profit margins are potentially overvalued, while firms with low revenue multiples and high profit margins may be undervalued.

methods_all.name: Value/Sales and Margins Matrix
methods_all.description: Plot firms in a sector or industry on this matrix to identify potential mismatches between margins and revenue multiples.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Compare current profit margins with expected future margins, consider the linearity of revenue multiples relative to margins, and account for risk factors.

methods_all.name: EV-to-sales ratios analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing EV-to-sales ratios against operating margins.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Firms with higher operating margins tend to have higher value-to-sales ratios.

methods_all.name: price-to-sales ratios analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing price-to-sales ratios against net margins.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Internet retailers' price-to-sales ratios plotted against their most recent year's net margins.

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Used to control for differences in risk, growth, and payout ratios across firms when analyzing revenue multiples.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Y = a + bX

methods_all.name: Regression Approach
methods_all.description: Uses regression analysis to predict value-to-sales ratios based on operating margins, risk, and growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: value-to-sales ratio = a + b1*Operating Mgn + b2*σOpInc + b3*Growth

methods_all.name: Cash Burn Ratio
methods_all.description: Measures potential cash problems in firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cash Balance / |EBITDA|

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Relates price-to-sales ratios to revenues, cash burn ratio, and revenue growth for Internet firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price-to-Sales Ratio = f(ln(Revenues), Cash Burn Ratio, Revenue Growth)

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Used to assess the relationship between price-to-sales ratio and net margins.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price-to-Sales Ratio = f(Net Margin)

methods_all.name: regression analysis
methods_all.description: Regressing price-to-sales ratios against net margins to determine valuation.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: N/A

methods_all.name: Price-to-Sales Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Regressing price-to-sales ratio against net margins and other fundamental variables.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PS = a + b1*Payout - b2*Beta + b3*EGR + b4*Margin

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis for EV/Sales
methods_all.description: Uses regression analysis to predict EV/Sales ratios based on financial metrics.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EV/Sales = a + b1(Expected growth) + b2(Operating margin) - c(Tax rate) + d(Reinvestment rate)

methods_all.name: EV-to-sales ratio valuation
methods_all.description: Estimate the future value of a firm by comparing its EV-to-sales ratio to industry averages.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value per share = (Future revenues * Industry average EV-to-sales ratio - Debt + Cash) / Shares outstanding

methods_all.name: Regression analysis for EV/sales ratios
methods_all.description: Use regression analysis to predict the future EV-to-sales ratio of a firm based on its expected growth rate and operating margin.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Predicted EV-to-sales = Regression equation (expected growth rate, operating margin) * Future revenues

methods_all.name: Sector-specific multiples
methods_all.description: Estimate value based on operating details and output.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Enterprise Value / Operating Units

methods_all.name: Enterprise Value per Unit
methods_all.description: Standardizes market value by dividing by units of product or service.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Enterprise Value / Units (e.g., tons of steel, subscribers)

methods_all.name: Subscriber Valuation Model
methods_all.description: Calculates the value of a firm based on existing and potential subscribers.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (V = NX * (VX - C) / r, where V is the total value of the firm, NX is the number of existing subscribers, VX is the net cash flow per subscriber, C is the cost to acquire a new subscriber, and r is the discount rate or cost of capital)

methods_all.name: Subscriber Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimating the value added by each subscriber over time.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value per Subscriber = (Revenues - Costs) / Number of Subscribers

methods_all.name: Cascading Values
methods_all.description: Evaluating companies based on value per user/member.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Compare EV/sales multiple and value/member to assess valuation.

methods_all.name: Price-Sales Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Calculates the appropriate price-sales multiple for a company based on its financial metrics.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PS = (Dividend Yield + Growth Rate) / (Risk-Free Rate + Market Risk Premium - Dividend Yield)

methods_all.name: Price-Sales Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate price-sales ratio based on profit margins and expected growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PSR = (Earnings Margin * Growth Rate) / (Cost of Capital - Growth Rate)

methods_all.name: Value-to-Sales Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate the value-to-sales ratio based on expected growth in operating income.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: V/S = (Operating Income Growth Rate / Cost of Capital) * (1 + Operating Income Growth Rate / Cost of Capital)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Models
methods_all.description: Adapting DCF models to value financial service firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Consider traditional dividend discount model, cash flow to equity discount model, and excess return model.

methods_all.name: Relative Valuation
methods_all.description: Using multiples for relative valuation of financial service firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Identify appropriate multiples based on firm characteristics.

methods_all.name: Debt as Raw Material
methods_all.description: Financial service firms treat debt as a raw material rather than capital.

methods_all.name: Valuation Method for Financial Service Firms
methods_all.description: Value equity directly rather than the entire firm due to regulatory constraints and difficulty in measuring reinvestment.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Discount expected cash flows to equity investors at the cost of equity instead of using WACC.

methods_all.name: Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Values equity as the present value of expected dividends.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(D_t / (1 + r)^t) for t=1 to infinity, where D_t is dividend at time t and r is discount rate.

methods_all.name: Free Cash Flow to Equity Model
methods_all.description: Estimates the value of equity by discounting cash flows to equity investors.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(FCFE_t / (1 + r)^t) for t=1 to infinity, where FCFE_t is free cash flow to equity at time t and r is cost of equity.

methods_all.name: Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Calculates stock value based on present value of expected dividends.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: V = DPS_t / (k_e - g)

methods_all.name: Bottom-up Beta Calculation
methods_all.description: Uses average levered beta for comparable firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Beta = Average Levered Beta of Comparable Firms

methods_all.name: Dividend Estimation from Earnings
methods_all.description: Calculates expected dividends based on earnings and payout ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Expected Dividends = Expected Earnings * Payout Ratio

methods_all.name: Historical Growth Method
methods_all.description: Estimating earnings growth based on past performance.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use historical data to predict future earnings, but be cautious if the regulatory environment is changing.

methods_all.name: Analyst Estimates Method
methods_all.description: Using forecasts from financial analysts for publicly traded firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Consider analyst estimates for widely followed banks and insurance companies, though their long-term accuracy may not exceed historical growth.

methods_all.name: Fundamental Growth Model
methods_all.description: Estimates expected EPS growth based on retention ratio and ROE.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: G = (RR * ROE)

methods_all.name: Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Valuation method using dividends and growth rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value per share = (Expected dividend next year) / (Cost of equity - Growth rate)

methods_all.name: Cost of Equity Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimates the cost of equity using beta, risk-free rate, and country risk premium.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Equity = Risk-Free Rate + Beta * (Market Return - Risk-Free Rate) + Country Risk Premium

methods_all.name: Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Calculates present value of expected dividends over a period using cost of equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Present Value = Dividends / (1 + Cost of Equity)^t

methods_all.name: Dividend Discount Model Adaptation
methods_all.description: Valuing non-dividend paying financial service firms using DDM.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = Expected future dividends discounted back at the cost of equity.

methods_all.name: Capitalizing Employee Expenses
methods_all.description: A method to capitalize and amortize employee training costs over a specified period.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Identify amortizable life, collect prior year expenses, compute current year's amortization expense, adjust net income.

methods_all.name: Capitalization of Employee Expenses
methods_all.description: Adjust net income by capitalizing employee development expenses.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Net Income Adjusted = Net Income + Unamortized Portion of Employee Development Expenses

methods_all.name: Equity Capital Reinvestment
methods_all.description: Calculating the impact of retained earnings on a firm's ability to expand its loan portfolio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Net Income - Dividends = Retained Earnings; Retained Earnings increase equity capital, allowing for additional loans proportional to the initial leverage ratio.

methods_all.name: Cost of Equity Calculation
methods_all.description: Uses bottom-up beta and risk-free rate to calculate the cost of equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Equity = Risk-Free Rate + Beta * Equity Risk Premium

methods_all.name: Present Value of Dividends
methods_all.description: Estimates potential dividends based on regulatory capital ratio and calculates their present value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PV = ∑(Dividend_t / (1 + r)^t) for t=1 to 5, where r is the cost of equity

methods_all.name: Discounting Terminal Value
methods_all.description: Calculating the value of equity by discounting future dividends and terminal value back to present.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of Equity = Present Value of Dividends + Discounted Terminal Value

methods_all.name: Excess Return Model
methods_all.description: A model that values equity based on excess returns.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Equity Value = Equity Capital Invested + Expected Excess Returns

methods_all.name: Cost of Equity Calculation
methods_all.description: Uses beta, Treasury bond rate, and equity risk premium.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Equity = Risk Free Rate + Beta * (Market Return - Risk Free Rate)

methods_all.name: Expected Cash Flows Method
methods_all.description: Valuing a loan portfolio or insurance policies based on expected cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of loans = Summation of discounted future cash flows using fair market interest rate.

methods_all.name: Price-Earnings Ratio Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the price-earnings ratio based on expected growth rate, payout ratio, and cost of equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P/E = (Expected Growth Rate + Payout Ratio) / Cost of Equity

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Using statistical regression to predict PE ratios based on payout ratios and expected growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE ratio = a + b1*payout_ratio + b2*expected_growth

methods_all.name: Valuing a Company Based on Business Units
methods_all.description: Breaks down net profit by business and applies PE ratio based on competitors in each segment.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cumulate equity values across businesses to determine overall company valuation.

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Ratio and Return on Equity Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing the relationship between price-to-book ratios and returns on equity for banks.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PBV = Market Price / Book Value; ROE = Net Income / Shareholder's Equity

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Regress price-to-book ratios against return on equity, beta, and expected growth rates.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P/B = f(RoE, Beta, Expected Growth)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Model
methods_all.description: Incorporates regulatory effects into both discount rate and expected future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Adjust the discount rate for non-diversifiable regulatory risk; adjust expected growth rates based on regulatory restrictions.

methods_all.name: Relative Valuation Models
methods_all.description: Compares financial service firms under different regulatory regimes.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use multiples that vary due to regulatory differences when comparing across countries or business types.

methods_all.name: Subsidy Benefit Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the value of government subsidies using expected cash flows and risk-free rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of subsidy = ∑(Expected Cash Flows * (1 + Risk-Free Rate)^-t)

methods_all.name: Dividend Discount Model
methods_all.description: Estimates the value per share based on future dividends.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: V = D1 / (r - g)

methods_all.name: Price-to-Book Ratio Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing price-to-book ratios relative to industry peers.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P/B ratio = Market Price per Share / Book Value per Share

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Predicting price-to-book ratios using regression against returns on equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Y = a + bX, where Y is P/B ratio and X is ROE

methods_all.name: Loan Portfolio Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimating the value of a loan portfolio based on interest income and market rates.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = Present Value of Future Cash Flows discounted at market rate

methods_all.name: Excess Return Approach
methods_all.description: Valuing equity by estimating future excess returns over cost of equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Equity Value = Sum of Discounted Excess Returns

methods_all.name: Earnings Growth Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating future earnings based on historical data or projections.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Growth rate = (Current Earnings - Previous Earnings) / Previous Earnings

methods_all.name: Analyst Estimates
methods_all.description: Use consensus estimates for projected earnings growth.

methods_all.name: Fundamental Approach
methods_all.description: Estimate growth using return on equity and reinvestment rate.

methods_all.name: Net Operating Losses Tracking
methods_all.description: Track net operating losses to shield future income from taxes.

methods_all.name: Temporary vs. Long-Term Problem Analysis
methods_all.description: Determine whether negative earnings are due to temporary or long-term issues.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Temporary: One-time events, economic downturns; Long-Term: Strategic missteps, operational inefficiencies, excessive debt.

methods_all.name: Making the Call: Short-Term Versus Long-Term Problems
methods_all.description: Guidelines for analysts to distinguish between temporary and long-term financial issues.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Consider management credibility, information timeliness and detail, industry confirmation, and problem persistence.

methods_all.name: Earnings Normalization
methods_all.description: Adjust earnings to remove non-recurring expenses and estimate normal earnings.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Remove specific event costs, including tax benefits. Normalize high expense items relative to previous years or use prior operating margins.

methods_all.name: Adjust Expected Growth
methods_all.description: Adjusting expected growth rate to reflect cyclical changes.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use a higher growth rate in the near term if both firm's earnings and economy are depressed but expected to recover quickly. Reverse strategy if current earnings are inflated due to an economic boom.

methods_all.name: Valuing a Cyclical Company Using a Higher Growth Rate
methods_all.description: Estimating future earnings and growth rates for cyclical companies during economic recovery.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Discounting projected free cash flows at the cost of capital to determine enterprise value.

methods_all.name: Earnings Normalization
methods_all.description: A method to estimate what a firm would earn in a normal year.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Average earnings over prior periods or average return on investment/profit margins. For cyclical firms, consider an economic cycle of 5-10 years.

methods_all.name: Average income
methods_all.description: Averaging operating income from a period to normalize.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Sum of operating incomes over the period / number of years

methods_all.name: Industry average margin
methods_all.description: Applying industry's average pretax operating margin to estimate normalized operating income.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Average pretax operating margin * current year revenues

methods_all.name: Normalized Operating Income Calculation
methods_all.description: Averaging pretax operating margin from past years and applying it to current revenue.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Average Margin (1998-2008) = 7.33%; Normalized Operating Income = Average Margin * Current Revenue

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Calculation
methods_all.description: Estimating the cost of capital using debt ratio and pretax cost of debt.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Capital = (Debt * Cost of Debt) + (Equity * Cost of Equity)

methods_all.name: Normalized Income Method
methods_all.description: Evaluates stock value based on normalized income.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value per share = Normalized Income / Number of Shares Outstanding

methods_all.name: Normalized Commodity Price Valuation
methods_all.description: Valuing firms using an average commodity price over a cycle.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Average price of the commodity over a historical period is used to estimate firm value.

methods_all.name: Current Price with Option Value
methods_all.description: Using current commodity prices and adding option value for future production adjustments.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = Current Production Value + Option Value

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Analysis
methods_all.description: Calculates the present value of future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Present Value = Future Cash Flows / (1 + Cost of Capital)^n

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Used to quantify the relationship between operating income and oil price.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Operating Income = β0 + β1 * Oil Price per Barrel

methods_all.name: Cost of Equity Calculation
methods_all.description: Uses bottom-up beta, Treasury bond rate, and equity risk premium.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Equity = Risk-Free Rate + Beta * (Market Return - Risk-Free Rate)

methods_all.name: Normalized Earnings Calculation
methods_all.description: Adjusting earnings to reflect normal levels.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Earnings adjustments are necessary for accurate valuation, especially in cases of short-term negative impacts.

methods_all.name: Earnings Shift Analysis
methods_all.description: Determine if a firm's earnings shift is permanent or temporary.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If the shift is permanent, value the firm assuming it will never recover lost ground. If optimistic about recovery, assume traditional margins and growth rates.

methods_all.name: Golden Share Valuation
methods_all.description: A method to assess the impact of government control on a privatized firm's valuation.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value attached to firms with golden shares will be lower due to restrictions on management changes and efficiency improvements.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating firm value based on future cash flows discounted at the cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(Cash Flows_t / (1 + Cost_of_Capital)^t)

methods_all.name: Debt Ratio Adjustment
methods_all.description: Adjusting debt ratio over time to reduce financial risk and lower cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Linear reduction in debt-to-capital ratio from high levels (e.g., 83.85% to 50%) over a period, leading to a decrease in beta and pretax cost of debt.

methods_all.name: DCF with Probability of Default Adjustment
methods_all.description: Value a firm using DCF and adjust for the probability it will not survive as a going concern.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Adjust DCF value by (1 - probability of default)

methods_all.name: Probability of Bankruptcy Estimation
methods_all.description: Use statistical techniques like probits to estimate the probability of bankruptcy.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating future cash flows and discounting them to present value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: FCFF = EBIT(1 - Tax Rate) + Depreciation - CapEx - ΔNet Working Capital

methods_all.name: Terminal Value Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate terminal value using FCFF, cost of capital, and stable growth rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: TV = (FCFF * (1 + g)) / (WACC - g)

methods_all.name: Probability of Default Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculate probability of default based on bond rating or bond price.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: π_distress = 1 − (1 − π_annual)^n

methods_all.name: Default Proceeds Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate proceeds from selling assets at a percentage of book value minus liquidation costs.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Proceeds = Book Value * Sale Percentage - Liquidation Costs

methods_all.name: Distress-adjusted value
methods_all.description: Calculates equity value considering going concern and distress/default probabilities.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of equity = (Probability of Going Concern * Value in Going Concern) + (Probability of Distress/Default * Value in Default)

methods_all.name: Valuation Method
methods_all.description: Calculates the value of equity based on book and market values.

methods_all.name: Valuing product options
methods_all.description: Three approaches to valuing firms with patents.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: 1) Market value of product options + DCF valuation; 2) Higher growth rate in DCF analysis; 3) Option pricing model + DCF valuation.

methods_all.name: DCF Valuation for Young Start-Ups
methods_all.description: Valuation challenges and considerations for young start-up firms.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Valuation Adjustments
methods_all.description: Adjusting DCF valuation for cyclical firms and negative earnings.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Adjust earnings for cyclical fluctuations; consider liquidation value in distressed situations.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimates the value of an investment based on its expected future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t), where CFt is the cash flow in period t, and r is the discount rate.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)
methods_all.description: Estimate future cash flows and discount them to present value using cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: FCFF = EBIT * (1 - Tax Rate) + Depreciation - CapEx - Change in Net Working Capital

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimate future cash flows and discount them to present value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: FCFF = EBIT * (1 - Tax Rate) + Depreciation - CapEx - ΔNet Working Capital

methods_all.name: Probability-Weighted Valuation
methods_all.description: Adjust valuation for risk of bankruptcy.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Expected Firm Value = P(No Bankruptcy) * Firm Value(No Bankruptcy) + P(Bankruptcy) * Firm Value(Bankruptcy)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Models
methods_all.description: A method to value firms based on their capacity to generate cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = Present Value of Expected Future Cash Flows

methods_all.name: Life Cycle Valuation
methods_all.description: A method to assess a company's valuation based on its stage in the business life cycle.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Estimate future growth potential and value based on management competence, market presence, and comparable firms.

methods_all.name: Venture Capital Method
methods_all.description: Valuation method for private firms based on future earnings and multiples.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Earnings in a future year * P/E ratio of comparable public firms = Exit value; Discount exit value to present at target rate of return.

methods_all.name: Venture Capital Target Return Calculation
methods_all.description: Uses a high target return to account for risk and negotiate stake size.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Target Return = (Historical Experience + Judgment + Risk of Failure) * High Beta

methods_all.name: Use of Updated Information
methods_all.description: Utilize the most recent financial data, especially for firms with negative earnings and high revenue growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Prefer trailing 12 months' revenues and earnings over last fiscal year's figures.

methods_all.name: Revenue Growth Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating future revenue growth rates based on past performance and scaling down as revenues grow.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Start with a target revenue figure for the terminal year, then work backwards using historical growth rates and principles of decreasing growth rate over time.

methods_all.name: Operating Margin Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating future operating margins based on current industry averages.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use the pre-tax operating margin of automotive firms globally in a given year as a target for future margins.

methods_all.name: Sales-to-Capital Ratio
methods_all.description: Estimates how much additional revenue is generated per dollar of capital invested.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Reinvestment = Additional Revenue / Sales-to-Capital Ratio

methods_all.name: Imputed Return on Capital Calculation
methods_all.description: Estimate return on capital each year based on initial investment and reinvestment.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Initial capital = Balance sheet value in 2010; Subsequent years' capital = Previous year's capital + Reinvestment for the current year.

methods_all.name: bottom-up approach
methods_all.description: Estimate risk parameters by averaging comparable firms' data.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If no comparable firms exist, use financial characteristics of the firm.

methods_all.name: synthetic rating estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate cost of debt using expected interest coverage ratio based on future operating income forecasts.

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the cost of capital for a firm based on its debt and equity structure.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Debt = Pretax Cost of Debt * (1 - Tax Rate); Cost of Equity = Risk-Free Rate + Beta * Market Risk Premium; Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) = (E/V) * Re + (D/V) * Rd * (1-Tc)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow with Probability Adjustment
methods_all.description: Estimate DCF value for going concern scenarios and apply probability of survival.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = (DCF Value) * (Probability of Survival)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimates firm value by discounting future free cash flows to present value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Present Value of FCFF = ∑(FCFFt / (1 + r)^t), Terminal Value = (FCF(t+1) / (r - g)), where FCF is Free Cash Flow, r is the cost of capital, and g is the growth rate.

methods_all.name: Fully Diluted Approach
methods_all.description: Divide the value of equity by the fully diluted number of shares.

methods_all.name: Treasury Stock Approach
methods_all.description: Add the exercise proceeds from the options to the equity value before dividing by the fully diluted number of shares.

methods_all.name: Option Valuation Approach
methods_all.description: Use the dilution-adjusted Black-Scholes model with market price and standard deviation to calculate option value and then determine value per share.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimates the intrinsic value of a stock based on future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1+r)^t), where CFt is the cash flow in year t, r is the discount rate.

methods_all.name: Value per Share and Pretax Margin
methods_all.description: Calculates value per share based on pretax margin improvement.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: The value per share increases by approximately $4/share for every 1% improvement in pretax margins.

methods_all.name: valuation skills
methods_all.description: Reflects the analyst's ability to value young firms with negative earnings.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating the present value of expected future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t)

methods_all.name: Earnings Expectation Management
methods_all.description: Managers must understand and manage investor expectations to maintain credibility.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Understand why investors value the firm as they do, communicate realistic potential without overpromising.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimates future cash flows to determine intrinsic value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: DCF = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t)

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate the cost of capital for a firm over the next five years.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (Risk-Free Rate + Risk Premium) * (1 - Debt Ratio)

methods_all.name: Probability-Weighted Expected Return
methods_all.description: Calculate expected value considering probabilities of success and failure.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value if Success * Probability of Success + Value if Failure * Probability of Failure

methods_all.name: Venture Capital Target Rate of Return
methods_all.description: Determine the target rate of return for venture capitalists based on company stage.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: 30% to 70%, depending on early or late startup phase

methods_all.name: Scaling Inputs for Revenue Growth
methods_all.description: Adjust financial inputs to reflect recent revenue growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Scale all inputs by the ratio of current year revenues to previous year revenues.

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis for Cost of Equity
methods_all.description: Use regression analysis to estimate cost of equity based on historical returns.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Regress stock returns against market index returns over a past period (2-5 years)

methods_all.name: regression analysis
methods_all.description: Regress returns on a stock against returns on a market index over a past period.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Not specified in the text, but typically involves statistical methods like OLS regression.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Model
methods_all.description: Valuing a firm by discounting expected future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t)

methods_all.name: Accounting Beta
methods_all.description: Estimate beta using changes in accounting earnings against an equity index.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Regress changes in private firm's accounting earnings against changes in earnings for an equity index (e.g., S&P 500). The slope of the regression is the accounting beta.

methods_all.name: Fundamental Betas Regression
methods_all.description: Relates firm betas to observable variables like ROE, debt ratios, and earnings variability.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: beta = a + b1*ROE + b2*(FA/TA) + b3*DC + b4*g

methods_all.name: Adjusting unlevered betas
methods_all.description: Adjusting unlevered betas for financial leverage in private firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Levered beta = Unlevered beta * (1 + (1 - tax rate) * market debt-to-equity ratio)

methods_all.name: Bottom-Up Beta Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating beta for a private firm by comparing it to publicly traded firms in the same industry.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If the proportion of fixed costs is higher than typical, use a higher beta. Adjust levered beta using tax rate and debt-to-equity ratio.

methods_all.name: Total Beta Calculation
methods_all.description: Adjusts market beta to include nondiversifiable risk.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Total Beta = Market Beta / ρjm, where ρjm is the correlation coefficient between the firm and the market.

methods_all.name: Total Beta Approach
methods_all.description: Estimates the cost of equity for a private firm by adjusting beta for leverage and tax effects.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Unlevered Beta = Levered Beta / (1 + (1 - Tax Rate) * Debt/Equity Ratio)

methods_all.name: Build up approach
methods_all.description: Start with expected return from a conventional risk and return model, add premiums for small cap and illiquidity risks.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Expected Return = Base Return + Small Cap Premium (4-5%) + Illiquidity Premium

methods_all.name: Using recent borrowing interest rates
methods_all.description: Use the most recent borrowing rate as a proxy for cost of debt.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Debt = Recent Borrowing Interest Rate

methods_all.name: Industry average cost of debt
methods_all.description: Assume private firm's cost of debt will align with industry average after IPO.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Debt ≈ Industry Average Cost of Debt (post-IPO)

methods_all.name: Synthetic ratings from interest coverage ratios
methods_all.description: Estimate synthetic bond rating based on interest coverage ratio and apply default spread.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Rating = Table 24.1 mapping; Default Spread = Rating's typical premium over riskless rate

methods_all.name: Debt Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Use industry-average or target debt ratios for consistency in levered beta and cost of capital calculations.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If industry-average debt-to-equity ratio is used to estimate the levered beta, use the same for cost of capital. If target debt-to-equity ratio is used, apply it similarly.

methods_all.name: Cost of Equity Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the cost of equity using market beta, Treasury bond rate, and risk premium.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Equity = Risk-Free Rate + Beta * (Market Return - Risk-Free Rate)

methods_all.name: Tax-adjusted valuation
methods_all.description: Adjusting cash flows for different buyer tax rates.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of a private firm can vary across buyers due to differences in marginal tax rates.

methods_all.name: CAPM
methods_all.description: Used to estimate discount rates for publicly traded companies.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: required rate of return = risk-free rate + beta * (market return - risk-free rate)

methods_all.name: Adjusted Income Statement
methods_all.description: Adjusting financial statements to reflect economic reality.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Add imputed depreciation and wages, convert operating lease expenses into capitalized leases.

methods_all.name: Growth Rate Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating growth rate based on reinvestment rate and return on capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Expected Growth Rate = Reinvestment Rate * Return on Capital

methods_all.name: Terminal Value Calculation
methods_all.description: Uses liquidation value for assets when a firm is expected to close.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: TV = Liquidation Value of Assets

methods_all.name: Infinite Growth Assumption
methods_all.description: Assumes continued growth beyond a certain period, especially in larger private firms with professional management.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Growth Rate > 0% after initial projection period

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Calculates the present value of future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = CF / (r - g)

methods_all.name: Illiquidity Discount Factors
methods_all.description: Identifies factors affecting illiquidity discount.

methods_all.name: Illiquidity Discount Calculation
methods_all.description: Estimating the illiquidity discount based on empirical studies and regression analysis.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: RPRS = Restricted stock price/Unrestricted stock price = 1 − Illiquidity discount

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing the relationship between illiquidity discount and firm characteristics.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: RPRS = Restricted stock price/Unrestricted stock price; REV, RBRT, DERN, DCUST as variables

methods_all.name: Bid-Ask Spread Approach
methods_all.description: Estimating illiquidity discounts by comparing bid-ask spreads of publicly traded stocks to variables measurable in private businesses.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Regress the bid-ask spread against firm revenues and profitability status, then extend regression to a larger sample.

methods_all.name: Restricted Stock Approach
methods_all.description: Estimates the illiquidity discount based on revenue and a base discount.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Base discount for $10 million in revenues is 25%, additional discount of 3.75% for smaller revenues, resulting in an estimated discount of 28.75%. (Silber equation)

methods_all.name: Bid-Ask Spread Approach
methods_all.description: Estimates illiquidity discount using trading volume and financial metrics.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses Nasdaq stock data regressed against revenues, earnings status, cash as percent of firm value, and trading volume. For private firms, trading volume is set to zero.

methods_all.name: illiquidity discount estimation
methods_all.description: Estimates illiquidity discount using restricted stock approach and bid-ask spread approach.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Based on earnings positivity and cash-to-revenue ratio, estimate illiquidity discount.

methods_all.name: Valuation for Sale to a Private Entity
methods_all.description: Uses total beta and private business tax rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of equity based on total beta, cost of debt may reflect additional spread.

methods_all.name: Valuation for Publicly Traded Firm or IPO
methods_all.description: Uses market beta and corporate marginal tax rates.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of equity based on market beta, cost of debt based on synthetic rating.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Calculates the present value of future cash flows to determine equity value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PV = CF / (1 + r)^t

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating future cash flows and discounting them to present value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PV = CF / (1 + r)^t

methods_all.name: Valuation Method with Control Premium
methods_all.description: Subtracting equity options' value from total equity and dividing by shares outstanding.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of Equity - Value of Options / Shares Outstanding

methods_all.name: Control Issue Valuation
methods_all.description: Calculating the difference in firm valuation under optimal management versus current management to determine control premium.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (Value of Firm Under Optimal Management - Value of Firm Under Incumbent Management) * Proportion of Controlling Interest

methods_all.name: Valuing Venture Capital and Private Equity Stakes
methods_all.description: Adjusts valuation approach based on investor diversification level.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Total Beta = Portfolio Correlation with Market

methods_all.name: Beta Adjustment for Private Companies
methods_all.description: Adjust beta based on the correlation of the company's business with the market and the investor's portfolio diversification.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Perceived beta = 1 / Correlation; Cost of equity = Risk-free rate + Perceived beta * Equity risk premium

methods_all.name: Cash Inflow Valuation
methods_all.description: Determines firm value based on whether cash is added to or removed from the firm.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Firm Value = Operating Assets + Cash (if not taken out by existing owners)

methods_all.name: Private Company Valuation via Transaction Multiples
methods_all.description: Uses historical transaction data of similar private companies to estimate the value of a target company.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = (Transaction Value / Scaling Variable) * Target's Scaling Variable

methods_all.name: Scale to variables less affected by discretionary choices
methods_all.description: Focus on revenue multiples over earnings multiples due to lower manipulation risk.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use revenue-based multiples instead of earnings-based multiples.

methods_all.name: Value businesses, not equity
methods_all.description: Classify and use enterprise value multiples rather than equity multiples for private companies.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Enterprise Value = Market Value of Equity + Debt - Cash and Equivalents

methods_all.name: Start with a large dataset
methods_all.description: Collect transaction data from a wide range of companies to screen out suspicious transactions.

methods_all.name: Adjust for timing differences
methods_all.description: Make adjustments to the value of transactions to account for timing differences.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Reduce transaction prices from earlier periods by the market drop during that period.

methods_all.name: Adjusting for life cycle differences
methods_all.description: Account for the fundamental differences between public and private firms due to their lifecycle stage.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Consider growth potential, market establishment level when comparing public multiples with private firm valuation.

methods_all.name: Risk adjustment
methods_all.description: Adjust risk assessment based on investor diversification status.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Apply higher cost of equity for undiversified investors in private businesses compared to diversified investors in public companies.

methods_all.name: Liquidity discount
methods_all.description: Factor in the liquidity difference between public and private equities.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Adjust valuation downwards when applying public multiples to illiquid private business assets.

methods_all.name: Adjust for survival
methods_all.description: Account for higher risk of failure in private businesses.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use public company multiples but adjust downward to reflect the increased risk.

methods_all.name: Adjust for nondiversification
methods_all.description: Apply a higher cost of equity due to undiversified risks.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If average PE ratio for similar public companies is 12, apply a lower PE ratio for private firms with same characteristics.

methods_all.name: Adjust for illiquidity
methods_all.description: Estimate and incorporate illiquidity discounts in valuation multiples.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use techniques from intrinsic valuation to adjust public multiples for illiquidity.

methods_all.name: Adjusting public multiple for illiquidity
methods_all.description: Techniques to adjust the public multiple value for illiquidity.

methods_all.name: Silber regression
methods_all.description: A method to determine liquidity discounts for private sales.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses historical data on transaction prices and market values of similar firms.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimates the value of an investment based on its expected future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: V = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t), where CFt is the cash flow in period t, and r is the discount rate.

methods_all.name: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
methods_all.description: Calculates the cost of equity based on market risk.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Re = Rf + β(Rm - Rf), where Re is the expected return on investment, Rf is the risk-free rate, β is the beta coefficient, and (Rm - Rf) is the market risk premium.

methods_all.name: bid-ask spread equation
methods_all.description: Estimate the illiquidity discount.

methods_all.name: Acquisition Premium Calculation
methods_all.description: Determines the difference between acquisition price and market price prior to acquisition.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Acquisition Premium = Acquisition Price - Market Price Prior to Acquisition

methods_all.name: Acquisition Price Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing the price paid for an acquisition compared to initial offer and book value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Compare acquisition price with initial offer and adjusted book value of equity.

methods_all.name: Takeover Premium Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing the premiums paid in tender offers and mergers.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Premiums averaged 19% in the 1960s, 35% in the 1970s, and 30% between 1980-1985.

methods_all.name: Impact of Takeover Failures
methods_all.description: Evaluating stock price reactions to takeover failures.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Substantial number of target firms are taken over within 60 days post-failure, earning significant excess returns (50%-66%).

methods_all.name: Acquire Undervalued Firms
methods_all.description: Identify and acquire companies trading below their intrinsic value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Requires superior information or analytical tools, access to capital, and a proven track record of successful acquisitions.

methods_all.name: Acquisition Premium Calculation
methods_all.description: Determines if an acquisition creates value based on premium paid.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If the premium exceeds 33 percent of the market price, the acquisition will not create any value for the acquirer.

methods_all.name: synergy analysis
methods_all.description: Identifying potential value from combining two firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Evaluate economies of scale, pricing power, functional strengths combination.

methods_all.name: Financial Synergy
methods_all.description: Combination of firms with excess cash and high-return projects.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value increase from projects funded by excess cash.

methods_all.name: Debt Capacity Increase
methods_all.description: Combined earnings stability allows for higher borrowing limits.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Tax benefit from increased debt capacity, shown as higher cash flows or lower cost of capital.

methods_all.name: Synergy Value Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates whether a merger creates additional value beyond the sum of its parts.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: V(AB) > V(A) + V(B)

methods_all.name: Intrinsic Value Framework
methods_all.description: Actions that enhance value increase cash flows from existing assets, expected growth rates, length of the growth period, or reduce cost of capital.

methods_all.name: Ego Discount
methods_all.description: Adjusting firm valuation based on managerial self-interest.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If a strong-willed CEO is replaced, the future return on capital may improve.

methods_all.name: Identifying Undervalued Targets
methods_all.description: Use relative valuation or discounted cash flow approaches to identify undervalued firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Relative valuation: compare multiples (P/E, P/BV, etc.) against industry peers. DCF valuation: compare market price to estimated intrinsic value.

methods_all.name: Diversification Target Selection
methods_all.description: Select targets in businesses unrelated and uncorrelated with the acquiring firm's business.

methods_all.name: Status Quo Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimating the firm value with existing policies before considering control and synergy premiums.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of the firm = function of cash flows, growth in cash flows, high-growth period length, cost of capital

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates the cost of capital using beta, debt ratio, and risk premium.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cost of Capital = (Beta * Risk Premium) + Treasury Bond Rate - After-Tax Cost of Debt * Debt Ratio

methods_all.name: Free Cash Flow Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimates firm value based on future free cash flows discounted at the cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Firm Value = ∑(FCFF_t / (1 + WACC)^t) for t=1 to 5 + Terminal Value / (1 + WACC)^5

methods_all.name: Value of Control
methods_all.description: Calculates the additional value a firm could achieve through restructuring.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: The value of control is significant for firms operating below optimal levels, as it can lead to substantial increases in value through improved management and financial structure.

methods_all.name: Synergy Valuation Method
methods_all.description: Answering two fundamental questions to estimate the form and impact of synergy on value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: 1. What form is the synergy expected to take? Will it reduce costs or increase growth potential? 2. Does it influence cash flows, growth rates, growth period, or cost of capital?

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Techniques
methods_all.description: Estimate the value of synergies in mergers and acquisitions.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value the combined firm with synergy built in, then subtract the pre-acquisition values to find synergy value.

methods_all.name: Synergy Value Calculation
methods_all.description: Subtract the value of the target firm (with control premium) + value of the bidding firm preacquisition.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (Value of Target Firm with Control Premium) + (Value of Bidding Firm Pre-Acquisition)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating the intrinsic value of a company by discounting its future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t), where CFt is the cash flow in year t, and r is the discount rate.

methods_all.name: Merger Synergy Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimating the value added by synergies in a merger.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of synergy = Value of combined firm (with synergy) - Value of combined firm (without synergy)

methods_all.name: Present Value Calculation
methods_all.description: Estimate the present value of synergy in a merger or acquisition.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use the combined firm's cost of capital as the discount rate.

methods_all.name: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
methods_all.description: Calculates the cost of capital for a firm based on its debt and equity weights.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: WACC = (E/V) * Re + (D/V) * Rd * (1 - Tc)

methods_all.name: Cash Slack
methods_all.description: Firms with excess cash may acquire firms with capital constraints to finance profitable projects.

methods_all.name: Tax Benefits
methods_all.description: Combining firms can result in tax benefits from unused tax deductions and asset write-ups leading to higher depreciation tax savings.
