methods_all.name: Present Value Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculating the present value of tax benefits from higher depreciation.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PV = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t), where CFt is the cash flow in period t, and r is the discount rate.

methods_all.name: Debt Capacity Calculation
methods_all.description: Estimating firm value after a merger based on increased debt capacity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of the combined firm = Current Value + (Increase in Debt Capacity * Beta Adjustment Factor)

methods_all.name: Acquisition Valuation
methods_all.description: Evaluating the financial impact of acquisitions on stock prices and earnings per share.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If the price paid for an acquisition exceeds its fair market value, the acquiring firm's stock price will decline. An acquisition is considered accretive if it increases EPS but this does not necessarily reflect true value.

methods_all.name: Comparable Firm Selection
methods_all.description: Choosing firms for comparison in valuation.

methods_all.name: Multiple Selection
methods_all.description: Selecting a multiple such as P/E, P/B, or P/S ratios.

methods_all.name: Matching Discount Rates with Cash Flows
methods_all.description: Ensuring that the discount rate used reflects the riskiness of the cash flows being discounted.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use the target firm's cost of equity to discount its pretax cash flows, not the acquirer's cost of equity.

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Discounting
methods_all.description: Uses cost of capital to discount cash flows to equity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Always wrong and results in overvaluation.

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Estimation
methods_all.description: Use the target company's debt ratio and cost of debt to compute the cost of capital for valuing the target company.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Do not use the acquiring firm’s cost of debt or debt capacity.

methods_all.name: Multiple Bidders Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyzing stock returns around takeover announcements.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Market-adjusted stock returns for successful bidders in single-bidder takeovers are estimated to be 2%, while in contested takeovers, they are -1.33%.

methods_all.name: Setting the Exchange Ratio
methods_all.description: Determine a fair exchange ratio based on intrinsic values of firms' shares to avoid mispricing.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Exchange ratio = (Intrinsic value per share of target firm) / (Intrinsic value per share of acquiring firm)

methods_all.name: Exchange Ratio Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimates exchange ratio for acquisitions based on value of control and synergy.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses exchratio.xls spreadsheet

methods_all.name: Goodwill Measurement
methods_all.description: Assesses the impact of goodwill on deal valuation and cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Goodwill should not alter the fundamental value of a target company unless it qualifies for tax deductions.

methods_all.name: Restructuring Charges Analysis
methods_all.description: Categorizes restructuring charges into three groups based on their impact on earnings and cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: 1. Cash expenses reduce expected cash flows; 2. Noncash, tax-deductible expenses increase cash flows by reducing taxes; 3. Noncash, non-tax-deductible expenses affect earnings but not cash flows.

methods_all.name: Mergers Evaluation
methods_all.description: Evaluating mergers based on size difference and synergy type.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Firms of equal size have lower success probability than acquisitions by larger firms; cost-saving mergers perform better.

methods_all.name: Going Private Valuation
methods_all.description: Incorporating long-term value maximization into cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Increased efficiency from going private can be reflected in higher operating margins and return on capital, but limited access to financial markets may reduce future growth.

methods_all.name: Valuing a Leveraged Buyout
methods_all.description: Estimate free cash flow to the firm and discount it back at a cost of capital that varies from year to year.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Discount free cash flows using period-specific cost of capital.

methods_all.name: Cash Flow Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating cash flows to the firm by subtracting net capital expenditures and changes in working capital from after-tax operating income.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (After-Tax Operating Income) - (Net Capital Expenditures + Changes in Working Capital)

methods_all.name: Acquisition Value Analysis
methods_all.description: Evaluates acquisitions based on reasons and target firm characteristics.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value created = (Present value of future cash flows - Cost of acquisition)

methods_all.name: Value of Control
methods_all.description: Calculate the difference between target firm's valuation under current and better management.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value of Control = Value with Current Management - Value with Better Management

methods_all.name: Synergy Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimate value from operating and financial synergies after merger.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Total Synergy Value = Operating Synergy + Financial Synergy

methods_all.name: Merger Valuation
methods_all.description: Estimate value of firms before and after merger, considering growth rates, cost structures, and synergies.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis to estimate firm values under different scenarios.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimate the value of PMT Corporation's equity under different management scenarios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t), where CFt is cash flow in year t, and r is the discount rate.

methods_all.name: Leveraged Buyout (LBO) Analysis
methods_all.description: Project future cash flows for Boston Turkey to estimate equity value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cash Flow to Equity = Cash Flow to Firm - Interest Expense + Depreciation - Capital Expenditures

methods_all.name: Discounting with cumulative cost
methods_all.description: Adjusts discount rate for changes in cost of capital over time.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cash flow in year n is discounted using a compounded cost of capital from the present to year n.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Valuation
methods_all.description: Valuing cash-flow-producing assets by discounting expected future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t)

methods_all.name: CAPM
methods_all.description: Measures risk by beta relative to the market portfolio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Risk = Beta * Market Risk

methods_all.name: Price Index Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimating price indices for classes of real estate assets.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Not specified in the text.

methods_all.name: beta estimation
methods_all.description: Using a stock index as proxy for market portfolio to estimate betas of stocks.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Beta = Covariance(Stock, Market) / Variance(Market)

methods_all.name: Regression Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating risk parameters by regressing returns on a class of real estate investments against returns on a consolidated market portfolio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Not specified in text, but involves statistical regression techniques.

methods_all.name: Total Beta Calculation
methods_all.description: Adjusts beta for nondiversification risk.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Total Beta = Portfolio Correlation with Market * Property Class Beta

methods_all.name: Illiquidity Discount Method
methods_all.description: Valuing illiquid assets as if they were liquid and then applying an illiquidity discount.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Discount rate = Liquid asset discount rate - Illiquidity premium

methods_all.name: Survey Approach
methods_all.description: Estimating discount rates for real estate investments by surveying potential investors.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Not based on abstract models but on actual investor preferences and desired returns.

methods_all.name: Risk and Return Model
methods_all.description: Measures risk and estimates discount rates based on risk measures.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Expected return = f(risk measure)

methods_all.name: Cost of Debt Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate the cost of debt using either bank loan interest rates or a synthetic rating based on interest coverage ratio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use marginal tax rate for after-tax cost of debt. Debt ratio is proportion of funds raised from debt to total value of property.

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital vs Cost of Equity
methods_all.description: Determine discount rate based on whether cash flows are pre-debt or post-debt.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Use cost of capital for pre-debt cash flows, cost of equity for post-debt cash flows.

methods_all.name: Vacancy Rate Projection
methods_all.description: Estimate the percentage of space that will not be rented out.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Vacancy rate = (Unrented space / Total space) * 100%

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Value Calculation
methods_all.description: Calculate the value of a building based on its future cash flows, considering occupancy time and lease terms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: DCF Value = ∑(Cash Flows_t / (1 + r)^t) for t=0 to n

methods_all.name: Lease Term Analysis
methods_all.description: Analyze the impact of lease length and provisions on cash flow estimates.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Net Lease: Tenant pays taxes, insurance, maintenance

methods_all.name: Inflation-based Terminal Value
methods_all.description: Estimate terminal value by increasing current asset value at expected inflation rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: terminal value = current value × (1 + inflation rate)^n

methods_all.name: Perpetual Growth Model

methods_all.name: Cost of Delay Calculation

methods_all.rules_or_formula: Each year of delay translates into one less year of value-creating cash flows. If cash flows are evenly distributed over time, and the life of the patent is n years, the cost of delay can be written as: (n - t) * annual_cash_flow

methods_all.name: Cost of delay calculation

methods_all.rules_or_formula: If the project rights are for n years, the annual cost of delay is calculated as a percentage of the total value. For evenly distributed cash flows: Cost of Delay = (1/n) * 100%. If uneven, it's defined by the next period’s cash flow as a percent of present value.

methods_all.name: Reinvestment Rate Impact
methods_all.description: Analyzing how changes in reinvestment rates affect the value per share.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: If a firm's return on capital exceeds its cost of capital, increasing the reinvestment rate positively impacts equity value. Conversely, if the return on capital is below the cost of capital, an increase in reinvestment rate negatively affects equity value.

methods_all.name: Growth and Margin Trade-off
methods_all.description: Analyzing the trade-off between revenue growth and operating margins.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Revenue Growth + Operating Margins = Total Value Creation

methods_all.name: Sales-to-Capital Ratio Impact
methods_all.description: Calculating the effect of sales-to-capital ratio on reinvestment needs and cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Higher Sales-to-Capital Ratio -> Lower Reinvestment Needs -> Higher Cash Flows

methods_all.name: Increasing Value Through Competitive Advantages
methods_all.description: Firms can increase value by extending high-growth periods with excess returns through barriers to entry and nurturing competitive advantages.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = f(Barriers_to_Entry, Excess_Returns_Period)

methods_all.name: Switching Costs
methods_all.description: A strategy that makes it difficult for customers to switch from a company's products to those of competitors.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: None provided, but involves making conversion processes seamless while increasing barriers for switching to competitors.

methods_all.name: Economies of Scale
methods_all.description: Larger firms can reduce costs per unit through increased production volume.

methods_all.name: Exclusive Distribution Rights
methods_all.description: Firms with exclusive rights to a distribution system have an advantage over competitors in attracting customers.

methods_all.name: Reducing Operating Risk
methods_all.description: Making products and services less discretionary to customers.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Lowering unlevered beta (cost of equity) and cost of debt default risk.

methods_all.name: Reduce Operating Leverage
methods_all.description: Decreasing the proportion of fixed costs in a firm's cost structure.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Tying expenses to revenues, using outside contractors for services, leading to lower unlevered beta, reduced cost of debt and increased optimal debt ratio.

methods_all.name: Change Financing Mix
methods_all.description: Adjusting the mix of debt and equity financing.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Debt is cheaper than equity due to tax advantages but increases risk; firm value maximization must consider impact on operations and cash flows.

methods_all.name: firm value maximization
methods_all.description: replaces cost of capital minimization as objective when financing mix is based on maximizing firm value.

methods_all.name: Debt Matching
methods_all.description: Matching cash flows on debt to cash flows on the asset.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Reduce risk of default, increase capacity to carry debt, reduce cost of capital, and increase firm value.

methods_all.name: Miller-Modigliani Theorem Application
methods_all.description: Understanding how real-world factors like taxes and default risk affect the theorem's assumptions.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: In a world with taxes and default risk, changing financing mix can affect firm value due to tax advantages and bankruptcy costs.

methods_all.name: Value Trade-off Analysis
methods_all.description: Determine if an action is a pure value creator or involves trade-offs.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Evaluate actions based on their impact on cash flows, cost of capital, and growth. Determine net effect on firm's value.

methods_all.name: Value Enhancement Chain
methods_all.description: Categorizes actions based on their impact and control over outcome.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Quick Fixes: Immediate value creation with firm control; Odds On: Near/medium term value creation with significant firm control; Long Term: Strategic initiatives for long-term value.

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Calculation
methods_all.description: Estimating the cost of capital for a company at various debt ratios.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: The cost of capital is calculated using a weighted average of the cost of equity and the after-tax cost of debt, adjusted for changes in the firm's leverage.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Analysis
methods_all.description: Calculating the value of equity based on future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value per share = Equity Value / Number of Shares Outstanding

methods_all.name: Cost of Capital Analysis
methods_all.description: Calculates equity value based on cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Equity Value = Present Value of Future Cash Flows discounted at Cost of Capital

methods_all.name: diagnose specific problems
methods_all.description: Identify unique issues faced by a firm.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Evaluates the value of a firm by discounting future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimate the value of a company by discounting its future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t)

methods_all.name: Growth Rate Estimation
methods_all.description: Estimate expected growth in operating earnings based on reinvestment rates and margins.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Growth rate = (Operating margin * Reinvestment rate) / Cost of capital

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA)
methods_all.description: Measures a firm's economic profit by subtracting the cost of capital from after-tax operating profits.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EVA = NOPAT - (WACC * Capital Invested)

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA)
methods_all.description: measures the dollar surplus value created by a firm on its existing investment

methods_all.name: Cash Flow Return on Investment (CFROI)
methods_all.description: measures the percentage return made by a firm on its existing investments

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA)
methods_all.description: A measure of dollar surplus value created by an investment or portfolio.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EVA = (Return on Capital - Cost of Capital) * Capital Invested

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA)
methods_all.description: A measure that adjusts for operating leases and R&D expenses to estimate after-tax operating income.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EVA = NOPAT - WACC * Capital Invested

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA) Approach
methods_all.description: Calculates firm value by summing the capital invested in assets, EVA from existing assets, and PV of future investments' EVA.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value = Capital Invested + EVA from Assets + PV(EVA from Future Investments)

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA)
methods_all.description: Measures the difference between operating income and the cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EVA = NOPAT - (Capital * WACC)

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_all.description: Estimating future cash flows and discounting them to present value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Present Value = Future Cash Flows / (1 + Cost of Capital)^n

methods_all.name: EVA valuation
methods_all.description: Calculates present value of EVA over a period and terminal year.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PV(EVA) = ∑(EVA_t / (1 + WACC)^t) for t=1 to 10, PV(Terminal EVA) based on imputed capital invested.

methods_all.name: EVA valuation
methods_all.description: A method to assess firm value based on economic value added.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EVA = NOPAT - (Capital Invested * Cost of Capital)

methods_all.name: DCF valuation
methods_all.description: Discounted Cash Flow analysis for valuing a project or company using future cash flows discounted by the weighted average cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: DCF = ∑(CFt / (1 + r)^t) + Terminal Value / (1 + r)^n

methods_all.name: EVA Valuation
methods_all.description: Calculates firm value based on economic value added from assets in place and new investments.

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA)
methods_all.description: Measures a company's financial performance after deducting the cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (Revenues - Costs) - (Capital Invested x WACC)

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA) Compensation

methods_all.rules_or_formula: EVA = NOPAT - (WACC * Invested Capital)

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA)
methods_all.description: Measures a company's financial performance after deduction of cost of capital
methods_all.rules_or_formula: EVA = NOPAT - (WACC * Invested Capital)

methods_all.name: CFROI
methods_all.description: Calculates the internal rate of return on existing investments based on real cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: CFROI = IRR(GCF, GI, n, SV)

methods_all.name: CFROI calculation
methods_all.description: Calculates return on investment using gross investment and salvage value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (Gross Cash Flow - Salvage Value) / (Gross Investment - Salvage Value)

methods_all.name: CFROI approach
methods_all.description: Modifies cash flow return on investment to account for growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses both past and future cash flows, unlike IRR which is based solely on expected future cash flows.

methods_all.name: Discounted Cash Flow Model
methods_all.description: Links CFROI with firm value through a stable growth model.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: FCFF = (GI - DA + CX - ΔWC) * (1 + g_n) / (k_c - g_n), where FCFF is free cash flow, k_c is cost of capital, and g_n is stable growth rate.

methods_all.name: CFROI
methods_all.description: Calculates the Cash Flow Return on Investment.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PV = CFROI_aip * GI_aip / (k_c - CFROI_aip)

methods_all.name: Present Value of Excess Cash Flows
methods_all.description: Calculates the present value based on future investments' CFROI and real cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PV = Σ (CFROI_t,NI - k_c) / (1 + k_c)^t

methods_all.name: Reduce gross investment
methods_all.description: Decrease investment in existing assets to boost CFROI.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: CFROI = (Cash Inflows - Cash Outflows) / Gross Investment

methods_all.name: Sacrifice future growth
methods_all.description: Prioritize current asset performance over potential future growth opportunities.

methods_all.name: CFROI
methods_all.description: Calculates the spread between cash flow return on investment and cost of capital.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: CFROI = (Cash Flow / Invested Capital) - Cost of Capital

methods_all.name: Economic Value Added (EVA)

methods_all.rules_or_formula: Steps include identifying root nodes, decision nodes, event nodes, and end nodes.

methods_all.rules_or_formula: Cumulative present value is calculated using the cost of capital as the discount rate. Expected values are computed by working backwards through the tree.

methods_all.rules_or_formula: Value firm based on expected cash flows after margin improvement.

methods_all.description: Assume high revenue growth and improving operating margins for young start-up companies.

methods_all.name: Steady state models
methods_all.description: Models that assume constant growth for stable-growth firms.

methods_all.name: Three-stage or n-stage models

methods_all.description: Use the variable most highly correlated with firm's value.

methods_all.name: Statistical approach
methods_all.description: Run regressions of each multiple against fundamentals to determine R-squared.

methods_all.name: conventional multiple approach

methods_all.description: method to estimate cost of equity and debt.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses weighted average of cost of debt and equity, adjusted for taxes and risk.

methods_all.name: DCF valuation
methods_all.description: A method to estimate the intrinsic value of an asset based on its expected future cash flows.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Present Value = Future Cash Flows / (1 + Discount Rate)^n

methods_all.name: Discounted terminal value
methods_all.description: Valuation method for young/start-up companies.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses a discount rate to estimate future cash flows.

methods_all.name: Distress-adjusted discounted cash flow (DCF) valuation
methods_all.description: Adjusts DCF models for distressed firms.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Incorporates distress risk into the discount rate and terminal value.

methods_all.name: F model
methods_all.description: A valuation method.

methods_all.name: FCFF model
methods_all.description: Free Cash Flow to the Firm model.

methods_all.name: Earnings Forecaster (Standard & Poor's)
methods_all.description: Tool for forecasting earnings.

methods_all.name: Enterprise value to EBITDA multiples
methods_all.description: Ratio used in valuation analysis.

methods_all.name: PE ratio
methods_all.description: Price-to-Earnings ratio.

methods_all.name: Economic value added (EVA)
methods_all.description: Measure of a company's economic profit.

methods_all.name: Discounted cash flow
methods_all.description: Method for valuing a project, company or asset based on its expected future cash flows.

methods_all.name: Efficient markets hypothesis
methods_all.description: Theory that financial markets reflect all available information in asset prices.

methods_all.name: E model (three-stage FCFE model)
methods_all.description: A three-stage Free Cash Flow to Equity valuation model.

methods_all.name: Expected growth. price-earnings (PE) ratio
methods_all.description: Calculates the PE ratio based on expected extraordinary growth.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PE = Expected Earnings / Current Stock Price

methods_all.name: adjusted present value approach
methods_all.description: valuation method that considers the tax benefits of debt.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: APV = NPV + PV(tax shield)

methods_all.name: Gordon growth model
methods_all.description: A valuation method used to determine the intrinsic value of a stock based on a future series of dividends that grow at a constant rate.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: P = D1 / (r - g)

methods_all.name: price–book value ratio
methods_all.description: Compares a stock's market price to its book value.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Price / Book Value

methods_all.name: price-earnings ratio
methods_all.description: Measures the relationship between a company’s stock price and earnings per share.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Stock Price / Earnings Per Share

methods_all.name: price-to-sales ratio
methods_all.description: Compares a company's market capitalization to its sales.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Market Capitalization / Total Sales

methods_all.name: two-stage dividend discount model
methods_all.description: A valuation method that accounts for changing growth rates over time.

methods_all.name: economic value added
methods_all.description: Measures a company’s economic profit.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: NOPAT - (WACC * Capital Invested)

methods_all.name: historical market beta
methods_all.description: A measure of a stock's volatility relative to the overall market based on historical data.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Covariance(Stock Returns, Market Returns) / Variance(Market Returns)

methods_all.name: historical risk premiums
methods_all.description: The difference between expected return and risk-free rate over time.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (Expected Return - Risk-Free Rate)

methods_all.name: implied equity premium
methods_all.description: Estimates the additional return investors expect for taking on extra risk in equities.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Historical Returns - Risk-Free Rate

methods_all.name: internal rate of return (IRR)
methods_all.description: The discount rate that makes the net present value of all cash flows from a particular project equal to zero.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: (NPV = 0) => IRR

methods_all.name: Log-linear regression models
methods_all.description: A statistical method for modeling relationships between variables.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Y = a + bX

methods_all.name: mean-variance models
methods_all.description: measuring market risk and asset performance.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Uses variance-covariance matrix to quantify risk and return trade-off.

methods_all.name: arbitrage pricing model
methods_all.description: explaining returns based on multiple factors.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: RP = α + β1F1 + β2F2 + ... + βnFn + εP

methods_all.name: Modified interest coverage ratio
methods_all.description: A financial metric used to assess a company's ability to pay its debt obligations.

methods_all.name: Modified multiples
methods_all.description: Adjusted valuation multiples that account for specific firm characteristics or market conditions.

methods_all.name: DCF valuation model
methods_all.description: Used for valuing office buildings, NYSE Composite.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Present value of future cash flows discounted at the appropriate rate.

methods_all.name: Net present value (NPV)
methods_all.description: Estimation method for future projects.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Sum of the present values of all expected future cash inflows and outflows.

methods_all.name: Option pricing model
methods_all.description: Used to determine the fair price of an option.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Black-Scholes, binomial models

methods_all.name: Ordinary least squares (OLS) regression
methods_all.description: Statistical method for estimating the relationship between variables.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Linear regression analysis

methods_all.name: Price-to-sales (PS) ratios
methods_all.description: Used for high-growth firm valuation.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: PS = Market Value of Equity / Total Sales

methods_all.name: Quick ratios
methods_all.description: Measures short-term liquidity.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Current Assets - Inventory / Current Liabilities

methods_all.name: Price-to-earnings ratio
methods_all.description: Evaluates the relative value of a stock based on its earnings.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: Market Price per Share / Earnings Per Share

methods_all.name: Retention ratio
methods_all.description: Calculation following firms in process of.

methods_all.name: Return on assets (ROA)
methods_all.description: Explanation of accounting for.

methods_all.name: Return on capital (ROC)
methods_all.description: Explanation of accounting for.

methods_all.name: Return on equity (ROE)
methods_all.description: Determinants of, effect of changing, explanation of accounting for.

methods_all.name: decision tree analysis
methods_all.description: method for risk management

methods_all.name: scenario analysis
methods_all.description: evaluation of best and worst case scenarios

methods_all.name: Monte Carlo simulations
methods_all.description: A method for assessing risk by generating a large number of possible outcomes and their probabilities.
methods_all.rules_or_formula: None specified, but involves statistical sampling techniques.

takeaways: Valuation is subjective and influenced by bias, which can be mitigated but not entirely eliminated.
Valuations must be updated regularly to reflect new information and changing market conditions.
Valuation is most crucial in uncertain and young companies, but overly complex models can introduce errors.
Approach valuation with skepticism, recognizing the difficulty of finding true mispricings in the market.
Valuation is crucial for fundamental analysts and franchise buyers, but less so for technical analysts.
Valuation plays a crucial role in various investment strategies, from determining support and resistance levels to predicting stock price movements based on new information.
Valuation in corporate finance is crucial for decision-making but inherently subjective and influenced by biases.
Valuation models are crucial for identifying market inefficiencies and setting price boundaries.
Discounted cash flow valuation is the foundational approach for understanding and applying other valuation methods.
DCF valuation aims to estimate an asset's intrinsic value by discounting expected future cash flows at a rate that reflects their riskiness.
Equity valuation discounts cash flows to equity at the cost of equity, while firm valuation uses WACC for all residual cash flows.
Mismatching cash flows and discount rates leads to overvaluation or undervaluation of equity and firm.
The Cost of Capital Approach and APV Approach provide consistent estimates if assumptions about cash flows and risk are consistent.
Discounted cash flow valuation is most applicable to firms with positive and reliably estimable future cash flows.
Discounted cash flow valuation must be adjusted for unutilized assets, patents, restructuring, acquisitions and private firm risks.
Valuing firms requires adapting models to fit the unique characteristics of private businesses and using relative valuation when intrinsic value is hard to determine.
Relative valuation involves comparing a company's multiples to those of its peers or historical levels, while DCF valuation focuses on intrinsic value based on expected future cash flows.
Multiples are a simple but subjective tool for valuation, requiring careful consideration of firm fundamentals and market conditions.
DCF valuation is less likely to be influenced by market errors compared to using multiples of comparable firms.
Option pricing models can be used to value assets whose payoffs are contingent on specific events or conditions.
Option pricing models are crucial for valuing assets with embedded options, but their applicability varies based on whether the underlying asset is traded or not.
Discounted cash flow valuation is a fundamental method that requires careful consideration of the discount rate.
Use DCF analysis and industry average PE ratios for firm valuation when market data is limited.
Financial statements provide essential information for analyzing and answering valuation questions.
Accounting statements provide a limited view of asset values and uncertainties.
Accountants generally prefer book value over market or estimated values due to reliability concerns.
Firms often use different depreciation methods for financial reporting versus tax purposes to manage earnings.
Different inventory valuation methods under GAAP can significantly impact a company's financial statements.
FIFO, LIFO, and weighted average are GAAP-compliant inventory valuation methods with distinct impacts on financial statements during inflation.
The choice between FIFO and LIFO inventory accounting methods can significantly impact a firm's reported financial performance due to tax benefits.
The equity approach is used for minority stakes (20-50%) while the consolidation method applies to majority stakes (>50%).
Goodwill is a critical component of acquisitions, reflecting the premium paid over book value and subject to annual impairment testing under current regulations.

Boeing's accounting practices, particularly in goodwill amortization and customer financing classification, reflect historical acquisition methods that may not be permissible under current regulations.
Accounting principles strictly categorize financing into debt or equity based on obligation nature, and value using historical costs with adjustments.
Accountants measure liabilities primarily at historical costs or book values, with adjustments for current conditions.
The classification of a lease as an operating or capital lease significantly impacts financial reporting and analysis.
Accurate classification of leases and proper funding of pension obligations are crucial for financial statement integrity.
Deferred taxes should be recognized in financial statements as a liability to reflect future payment obligations.
The book value of equity can be significantly affected by short-term stock buybacks, long-term losses, and unrealized gains/losses in marketable securities.
Accrual accounting recognizes revenue and expenses in the period they are incurred, providing a more accurate measure of earnings than cash-based systems.
Understanding the distinction between operating and nonrecurring expenses is crucial for accurate earnings forecasting.
Return on Assets (ROA) measures a firm's operating efficiency in generating profits from its assets, providing insight into operational performance independent of financing effects.
Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) offers a more accurate measure of return and can be compared to the cost of capital.
The return on capital varies widely across firms due to differences in profit margins and capital turnover ratios.

The text covers various financial metrics and their applications in investment analysis, particularly focusing on PBV and PE ratios.
PS ratios and probabilistic valuation are crucial for evaluating high-growth and private equity firms.
Understanding real estate valuation methods such as discounted cash flow and relative valuation is crucial for accurate investment analysis.
Understanding key financial ratios like ROA, ROC, and ROE is crucial for investment analysis.
Scenario and sensitivity analyses are crucial for assessing risks in investment decision-making.
Monte Carlo simulations are a powerful tool for assessing risk and decision making in investment.
Tax factors and effective tax rates are critical in the valuation of acquisitions and takeovers.

methods_unique.name: Discounting biased equity research
methods_unique.description: Portfolio managers and investors should discount or ignore equity research that creates potential for bias.

methods_unique.name: Parsimony Principle
methods_unique.description: Adhere to using only necessary inputs in valuation models.

methods_unique.name: Skepticism towards market efficiency
methods_unique.description: Approach the issue of market efficiency with skepticism, recognizing that markets make mistakes but finding these requires skill and luck.

methods_unique.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Models
methods_unique.description: A method to estimate the value of an investment based on its expected future cash flows.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Present Value = ∑(Cash Flow_t / (1 + r)^t)

methods_unique.name: valuation for support and resistance lines
methods_unique.description: Use valuation metrics to determine price levels where buying or selling pressure is expected.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Not specified in text, but involves technical analysis principles.

methods_unique.name: information trading
methods_unique.description: Trade based on anticipated market reactions to new information.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Analyze the relationship between information and stock price changes.

methods_unique.name: Synergy valuation
methods_unique.description: Quantitative assessment of synergies in mergers and acquisitions.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Consider the combined value of both firms, including potential cost savings and revenue enhancements.

methods_unique.name: valuation model application
methods_unique.description: Using a valuation model to determine stock value.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Compare the output of the valuation model with market price to identify potential inefficiencies or errors.

methods_unique.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) valuation
methods_unique.description: relates the value of an asset to the present value of expected future cash flows on that asset.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value = ∑(CFt / (1+r)^t), where CFt is the cash flow in period t, r is the discount rate, and n is the life of the asset.

methods_unique.name: Equity Valuation
methods_unique.description: Discounting expected cash flows to equity at the cost of equity.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of Equity = ∑(CF to equity_t / (1 + k_e)^t) for t=1 to n

methods_unique.name: Dividend Discount Model
methods_unique.description: A special case of equity valuation focusing on dividends.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of Equity = PV(Expected Future Dividends)

methods_unique.name: Firm Valuation
methods_unique.description: Discounting expected cash flows to the firm at WACC.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of Firm = ∑(CF to firm_t / (1 + WACC)^t) for t=1 to n

methods_unique.name: Discount Cash Flows to Equity at Cost of Equity
methods_unique.description: Calculate the value of equity by discounting cash flows to equity using the cost of equity.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of Equity = CFt / (1 + rE)^t, where rE is the cost of equity

methods_unique.name: Discount Cash Flows to Firm at Cost of Capital
methods_unique.description: Calculate the value of the firm by discounting cash flows to the firm using the weighted average cost of capital.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of Firm = CFt / (1 + WACC)^t, where WACC is the weighted average cost of capital

methods_unique.name: Cost of Capital Approach
methods_unique.description: Calculates the cost of capital for a firm using equity and debt.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Cost of capital = Cost of equity(Proportion of equity used to fund business) + Pretax cost of debt (1 – Tax rate)(Proportion of debt used to fund business)

methods_unique.name: Adjusted Present Value (APV) Approach
methods_unique.description: Separates the effects on value of debt financing from the value of the assets.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of business = Value of business with 100% equity financing + Present value of expected tax benefits of debt - Expected bankruptcy costs

methods_unique.name: Discounted Cash Flow Valuation
methods_unique.description: Calculates the present value of future cash flows.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of asset = Present value of excess return + Investment in the asset

methods_unique.name: Relative Valuation
methods_unique.description: Uses multiples of comparable firms to estimate value.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value = Comparable Firm's Multiple * Relevant Variable (e.g., earnings, cash flows) for the firm being valued

methods_unique.name: Relative Valuation Models
methods_unique.description: Comparing multiples of a company to its peers or historical levels.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Compare P/E, P/S, and other ratios with sector averages or past performance.

methods_unique.name: Cross-Sectional Comparisons
methods_unique.description: Comparing price-earnings ratio of a firm to the average P/E ratio of other firms in the same industry.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: P/E ratio comparison

methods_unique.name: Comparisons across Time
methods_unique.description: Comparing current multiples with historical multiples for mature firms.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Historical multiple comparison

methods_unique.name: liquidation value
methods_unique.description: Aggregate estimated sale proceeds of assets.

methods_unique.name: replacement cost
methods_unique.description: Estimate cost to replace all firm's assets today.

methods_unique.name: Black-Scholes Option Pricing Model
methods_unique.description: A model for valuing options based on underlying asset's current value, variance, strike price, time to expiration, and riskless interest rate.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: C = S*N(d1) - X*e^(-rT)*N(d2)

methods_unique.name: Option Pricing Model
methods_unique.description: Used to value assets with embedded options, such as undeveloped natural resource reserves.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Incorporates the right of the asset holder to develop or not based on market conditions.

methods_unique.name: Contingent Claim Valuation
methods_unique.description: Valuing an asset with option-like characteristics using option pricing models.

methods_unique.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis
methods_unique.description: Calculating firm and equity values using projected cash flows.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of Equity = Σ(Annual Cash Flows to Equity / (1 + Cost of Equity)^t); Value of Firm = Σ(Annual Cash Flows to the Firm / (1 + Cost of Capital)^t)

methods_unique.name: Book Value Principle
methods_unique.description: Starts with historical cost as the best estimate of value.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Unless a substantial reason is given, book value equals historical cost.

methods_unique.name: Market Value Skepticism
methods_unique.description: Views market or estimated values with suspicion due to volatility and manipulation risks.

methods_unique.name: Depreciation Methods
methods_unique.description: Straight line and accelerated depreciation methods for fixed assets.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Straight-line: constant annual depreciation; Accelerated: higher depreciation in early years, lower later.

methods_unique.name: Inventory Valuation Methods
methods_unique.description: GAAP allows FIFO, LIFO, and weighted average methods.

methods_unique.name: First in, first out (FIFO)
methods_unique.description: Cost of goods sold is based on the earliest material costs.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: COGS = cost of earliest materials; Inventory value approximates current replacement cost.

methods_unique.name: Last in, first out (LIFO)
methods_unique.description: Cost of goods sold is based on the most recent material costs.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: COGS = cost of latest materials; Inventory value reflects earlier purchase prices.

methods_unique.name: Weighted average
methods_unique.description: Both inventory and COGS are based on the average cost of all materials purchased during the period.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Average cost per unit = total cost of units / number of units; COGS and inventory values reflect this average.

methods_unique.name: LIFO vs FIFO
methods_unique.description: Inventory valuation methods with tax implications.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Firms use LIFO for tax benefits in high inflation periods, increasing cash flows and reducing taxable income.

methods_unique.name: Equity Approach
methods_unique.description: Used for minority active investments where ownership is between 20% and 50%. Adjusts acquisition cost based on net income, losses, and dividends received.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Proportional share of net income/losses + dividends received adjust the acquisition cost.

methods_unique.name: Consolidation Method
methods_unique.description: Used for majority active investments where ownership is more than 50%. Replaces financial investment with assets and liabilities of the firm, leading to balance sheet consolidation.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: 100% of subsidiary's revenues, earnings, and assets are consolidated into parent's financial statements; minority interest shown as liability.

methods_unique.name: Straight-line Amortization
methods_unique.description: A method to amortize intangible assets over their expected lives.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Amortization expense = Total cost of intangible asset / Expected useful life

methods_unique.name: Goodwill Amortization
methods_unique.description: Boeing amortizes goodwill from the Rockwell acquisition over 30 years.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Amortization period = 30 years

methods_unique.name: Customer Financing and Accounts Receivable
methods_unique.description: Boeing shows present value of future payments as customer financing, current portion as accounts receivable.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Present value of future payments classified as customer financing

methods_unique.name: Historical Cost Adjustments
methods_unique.description: Accounting method for valuing liabilities and equity.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Use historical costs with adjustments, not expected future cash flows or market value.

methods_unique.name: Historical Cost Accounting
methods_unique.description: Uses historical costs with adjustments for valuation of assets, liabilities and equities.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value = Historical Cost + Adjustments

methods_unique.name: Lease Accounting
methods_unique.description: Determines whether a lease is an operating or capital lease based on specific criteria.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: A lease must be treated as a capital lease if it meets any one of the following conditions: 1) The lease life exceeds 75 percent of the life of the asset; 2) There is a transfer of ownership to the lessee at the end of the lease term; 3) There is an option to purchase the asset at a bargain price at the end of the lease term; 4) The present value of the lease payments, discounted at an appropriate discount rate, exceeds 90 percent of the fair market value of the asset.

methods_unique.name: Lease Classification
methods_unique.description: Determine if a lease is capital or operating based on present value criteria.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Present value of lease payments exceeds 90% of fair market value for capital leases.

methods_unique.name: Pension Funding
methods_unique.description: Estimate and fund pension obligations accurately to avoid underfunding liabilities.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Defined benefit plans require estimating future benefits based on contributions, returns, and expected rates of return.

methods_unique.name: Deferred Taxes Calculation
methods_unique.description: Calculates deferred taxes based on difference between reported and actual tax paid.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Deferred Tax Liability = Reported Tax - Actual Tax Paid

methods_unique.name: Treasury Stock Method
methods_unique.description: Companies can show repurchased stock as treasury stock to reduce book value of equity.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Repurchased stock must be reissued or used for option exercises within a short period; otherwise, it reduces the book value of equity.

methods_unique.name: Accrual Accounting
methods_unique.description: Recognizes revenue and expenses in the period they are incurred, not when cash transactions occur.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Revenue = Sales; Expenses = Costs associated with generating sales

methods_unique.name: Return on Assets (ROA)
methods_unique.description: Measures operating efficiency in generating profits from assets.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Earnings before interest and taxes(1 – Tax rate)/Total assets or [Net income + Interest expenses(1 – Tax rate)]/Total assets

methods_unique.name: Pretax ROA
methods_unique.description: Measures profitability before tax adjustments.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Pretax ROA = Earnings before interest and taxes/Total assets

methods_unique.name: Return on Invested Capital (ROIC)
methods_unique.description: Evaluates return relative to the cost of capital, using book value of debt and equity net of cash as denominator.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: ROC or ROIC = Operating income / Invested capital

methods_unique.name: Return on Capital (ROC) Maximization
methods_unique.description: Calculates the optimal combination of profit margin and capital turnover to maximize ROC.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: ROC = Profit Margin x Capital Turnover

methods_unique.name: Return on Capital (ROC)
methods_unique.description: Measures profitability of overall firm
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: ROC = EBIT(1 – t)/(BV of debt + BV of equity) where EBIT is earnings before interest and taxes, t is tax rate

methods_unique.name: Return on Equity (ROE)
methods_unique.description: Examines profitability from the perspective of the equity investor
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: ROE = Net Income / Book Value of Equity

methods_unique.name: Return on Equity Decomposition
methods_unique.description: Estimates return on equity by breaking down into after-tax return on capital, debt-equity ratio, and book interest rate.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Return on Equity = After-Tax Return on Capital + (Debt-Equity Ratio * Book Interest Rate(1 - Tax Rate))

methods_unique.name: Current Ratio
methods_unique.description: Measures short-term liquidity risk.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Current assets / Current liabilities

methods_unique.name: Quick or Acid Test Ratio
methods_unique.description: A variant of the current ratio that excludes inventory and accounts receivable from current assets to measure liquidity.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: (Cash + Marketable Securities) / Current Liabilities

methods_unique.name: Turnover Ratios
methods_unique.description: Measure efficiency in working capital management by assessing how quickly a company converts its assets into sales or cash.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Accounts Receivable Turnover = Sales / Average Accounts Receivable; Inventory Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory

methods_unique.name: Interest Coverage Ratio
methods_unique.description: Measures the firm's capacity to meet interest payments from pre-debt, pretax earnings.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Higher ratio indicates better security in making interest payments.

methods_unique.name: Fixed Charges Coverage Ratio
methods_unique.description: Extends the denominator of the interest coverage ratio to include other fixed obligations such as lease payments.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Uses EBITDA in numerator and cash fixed charges in denominator.

methods_unique.name: Capital Expenditure Ratio
methods_unique.description: Captures extent of capital expenditures relative to operating cash flows.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Defined by measuring the cash flows from continuing operations, before interest but after taxes and after meeting working capital needs.

methods_unique.name: Cash Fixed Charges Coverage Ratio
methods_unique.description: Evaluates the cash flow adequacy of a company's ability to cover fixed charges.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Cash Fixed Charges Coverage Ratio = Cash Flows from Operations / Cash Fixed Charges

methods_unique.name: Debt to Total Capital Ratio
methods_unique.description: Measures debt as a proportion of the total capital of the firm.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Debt to Total Capital Ratio = Debt / (Debt + Equity)

methods_unique.name: Debt to Book Value of Equity Ratio
methods_unique.description: Measures debt as a proportion of the book value of equity in the firm.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Debt to Book Value of Equity Ratio = Debt / Book Value of Equity

methods_unique.name: Debt Ratio Calculation
methods_unique.description: Calculates the ratio of preferred stock to capital including debt, equity, and preferred stock.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Preferred Stock / (Debt + Equity + Preferred Stock)

methods_unique.name: Long-term Debt Ratio
methods_unique.description: Uses only long-term debt in the calculation.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Long-term Debt / (Debt + Equity + Preferred Stock)

methods_unique.name: Market Value Debt Ratio
methods_unique.description: Uses market value of debt and equity instead of book values.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: (MV of Long-term Debt + MV of Short-term Debt) / (MV of Equity + MV of Preferred Stock)

methods_unique.name: Depreciation and Amortization
methods_unique.description: Spreads capital expenditures over multiple periods.

methods_unique.name: Net Working Capital Estimation
methods_unique.description: Estimate the net working capital and noncash working capital for Coca-Cola.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: (Current Assets - Current Liabilities) - (Noncash Current Assets - Noncash Current Liabilities)

methods_unique.name: Capitalization of Advertising Expenses
methods_unique.description: Determining whether advertising expenses should be treated as operating or capital expenses.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Use the capitalization of R&D as a guide.

methods_unique.name: Return on Equity Calculation
methods_unique.description: Calculating return on equity using beginning book value of equity and average book value of equity.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: (a) Book Value of Equity at End of Year - Shares Repurchased * Price per Share + Net Income - Dividends = New Book Value of Equity; (b) Return on Equity = Net Income / Beginning Book Value of Equity; (c) Average ROE = Net Income / ((Beginning Book Value + Ending Book Value) / 2)

methods_unique.name: Current Ratio Adjustment
methods_unique.description: Determines the effect of equal increases in current assets and liabilities on the current ratio.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: If the initial current ratio is greater than 1, an equal increase in both will decrease it. If less than 1, such an increase will raise it.

methods_unique.name: Risk-Opportunity Trade-off
methods_unique.description: Measuring danger and converting it into opportunity.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Risk = Danger; Opportunity = Expected Return

methods_unique.name: Expected Return Calculation
methods_unique.description: Calculating expected return on an investment.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Expected Return = Sum of (Probability of Outcome * Return for that Outcome)

methods_unique.name: Expected Return and Variance Analysis
methods_unique.description: Measuring investment opportunity and danger with expected return and standard deviation.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Investors choose between two investments based on higher expected returns if the standard deviations are equal.

methods_unique.name: Skewness and Kurtosis Consideration
methods_unique.description: Evaluating investment risk beyond mean and variance by considering skewness and kurtosis.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Positive skewness and lower kurtosis distributions preferred over negative skewness and higher kurtosis.

methods_unique.name: Historical Risk Assessment
methods_unique.description: Evaluating risk based on historical data.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Standard deviations and variances of stocks in various sectors are used to measure risk.

methods_unique.name: Diversification
methods_unique.description: Investing in multiple assets to reduce firm-specific risk.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Portfolio variance = σ²A + σ²B + 2ρABσAσB

methods_unique.name: Mean-Variance Models
methods_unique.description: Measures market risk by considering the distribution of actual returns around expected return.

methods_unique.name: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
methods_unique.description: A model that measures nondiversifiable or market risk.

methods_unique.name: Arbitrage Pricing Theory
methods_unique.description: A model that breaks down risk into firm-specific and market components.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: R = E(R) + βj * Fj + ε

methods_unique.name: Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)
methods_unique.description: A theory that describes the relationship between expected return and risk for assets based on multiple factors.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: E(Rj) = Rf + β1(E(R1)-Rf) + β2(E(R2)-Rf) + ... + βk(E(Rk)-Rf)

methods_unique.name: Multifactor Model
methods_unique.description: Determines factors from historical data and correlates them with macroeconomic variables.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: E(R_GNP) = β_GNP * E(MRP_GNP), E(R_I) = β_I * E(MRP_I)

methods_unique.name: Power Law Distribution
methods_unique.description: A distribution where the relationship between two variables, Y and X, is described by a constant (α) and a power law exponent (k).
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Y = αX^(-k)

methods_unique.name: Power Law Exponents
methods_unique.description: A method to measure risk of large movements in assets.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Assets with higher exponents are considered less risky due to fewer extreme values.

methods_unique.name: Jump Process Models
methods_unique.description: Models incorporating stock price jumps using Poisson distribution.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Parameters include expected jump size (μ), variance in jump size (δ^2), and frequency of jumps (λ).

methods_unique.name: Regression or Proxy Models
methods_unique.description: Models that use observable market variables to explain returns, rather than economic theory assumptions.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Example: Fama-French model uses firm's market capitalization and price-to-book ratio as explanatory factors for stock returns.

methods_unique.name: Fama-French Proxy Model
methods_unique.description: Regression model that uses market capitalization and price-to-book ratio to predict stock returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Expected monthly return = α + β1(Size) + β2(Book-to-Market Ratio)

methods_unique.name: Data mining
methods_unique.description: Identifying variables related to returns from large datasets.

methods_unique.name: Standard error calculation
methods_unique.description: Evaluating the precision of historical premia in proxy models.

methods_unique.name: Fama-French three-factor model
methods_unique.description: Includes size and book-to-market betas to explain returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Expected return = Risk-free rate + Beta * Market risk premium + Size factor + Book-to-market factor

methods_unique.name: CAPM
methods_unique.description: Capital Asset Pricing Model for estimating expected returns based on beta.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: E(Ri) = Rf + βi(E(Rm) - Rf)

methods_unique.name: Cash Flow Coverage
methods_unique.description: Measures the magnitude of cash flows relative to obligations.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: CF / Obligations

methods_unique.name: Financial Ratios for Default Risk
methods_unique.description: Use financial ratios to measure default risk.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: EBITDA/Revenues, ROIC = EBIT/(BV of debt + BV of equity – Cash), Interest coverage ratio (EBIT/Interest expenses), EBITDA/Interest

methods_unique.name: ROIC
methods_unique.description: Return on Invested Capital calculation.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: EBIT/(BV of debt + BV of equity – Cash)

methods_unique.name: Debt-to-EBITDA ratio
methods_unique.description: Indicates the extent of financial leverage and debt repayment capacity.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: BV of Debt/EBITDA

methods_unique.name: Average Annual Return Calculation
methods_unique.description: Calculate the average annual return based on price changes and dividends.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: (Ending Value + Dividends - Beginning Value) / Beginning Value

methods_unique.name: Standard Deviation and Variance Calculation
methods_unique.description: Compute standard deviation and variance of annual returns to measure volatility.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Variance = Σ(Annual Return - Average Annual Return)^2 / n, Standard Deviation = √Variance

methods_unique.name: Portfolio variance calculation
methods_unique.description: Calculates the variance of a portfolio given individual asset returns, standard deviations, and correlations.

methods_unique.name: Expected Return and Standard Deviation Calculation
methods_unique.description: Calculates expected return and standard deviation after shifting funds between risky assets and risk-free asset.

methods_unique.name: Capital Asset Pricing Model
methods_unique.description: Determines the proportion of wealth invested in market portfolio and riskless asset based on desired standard deviation.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: σp = wM*σM, E(Rp) = wM*E(Rm) + (1-wM)*Rf

methods_unique.name: Covariance and Variance Estimation
methods_unique.description: Estimates covariance between two assets and their individual variances.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: cov(X,Y) = Σ[(Xt - Xavg)*(Yt - Yavg)]/n, Var(X) = Σ(Xt - Xavg)^2/n

methods_unique.name: Beta Estimation
methods_unique.description: Estimates the beta of an asset using market returns and covariance.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: β = cov(Ri,Rm)/Var(Rm)

methods_unique.name: Correlation Calculation
methods_unique.description: Calculates correlation between two assets given their standard deviations and covariance.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: ρ(X,Y) = cov(X,Y)/(σX*σY)

methods_unique.name: Arbitrage Pricing Model
methods_unique.description: Estimates expected return using factor betas and risk premiums.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: E(Ri) = Rf + β1*(Rm1-Rf) + β2*(Rm2-Rf) + ...

methods_unique.name: Power Law Distribution in Financial Market Fluctuations
methods_unique.description: Analyzing stock price fluctuations over time to identify patterns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: The exponent for stock returns is roughly 3.

methods_unique.name: Option Pricing Theory
methods_unique.description: Determines the value of options based on underlying asset values and strike prices.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value = Present Value of Expected Cash Flows + Contingent Events Adjustment

methods_unique.name: Call Option Payoff
methods_unique.description: Net payoff is gross payoff minus call price.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Net Payoff = Gross Payoff - Call Price

methods_unique.name: Put Option Payoff
methods_unique.description: Net profit from put option is gross profit minus initial cost of the put.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Net Profit = (Strike Price - Market Value) - Initial Cost

methods_unique.name: Option Pricing
methods_unique.description: Determines how changes in underlying asset value and variance affect call and put options.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Calls increase in value with asset price, puts decrease; higher variance increases option value for both calls and puts; dividends decrease call value and increase put value.

methods_unique.name: Option Value and Strike Price
methods_unique.description: In calls, higher strike price decreases value; in puts, higher strike price increases value.

methods_unique.name: Time to Expiration Impact
methods_unique.description: Both call and put values increase with longer time to expiration due to more potential for underlying asset movement.

methods_unique.name: Option Valuation
methods_unique.description: Calculates the value of options based on various factors.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Present value of exercise price, interest rates, time to expiration affect call and put values differently.

methods_unique.name: Binomial Model
methods_unique.description: A model that values options based on the probability of two possible stock price movements.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: S is the current stock price; Su with probability p and Sd with probability (1-p) in any time period.

methods_unique.name: Binomial Option Pricing Model
methods_unique.description: Uses risk-free borrowing/lending and underlying asset to replicate option cash flows.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: C = ΔSu + (1+r)^-t(B - ΔSd)

methods_unique.name: Black-Scholes Model
methods_unique.description: Model derived when binomial model's time periods approach zero with continuous price process.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Estimates the value of any option using a small number of inputs.

methods_unique.name: Present Value Adjustment for Short-term Options
methods_unique.description: Estimate and subtract the present value of expected dividends from the asset's current value.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: S = Current Asset Value - Present Value of Expected Dividends

methods_unique.name: Dividend Yield Adjustment for Long-term Options
methods_unique.description: Adjust the Black-Scholes model using a constant dividend yield if it remains unchanged over the option life.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: y = Dividends/Current value of the asset

methods_unique.name: Black-Scholes Model with Dividend Adjustments
methods_unique.description: Modifies the original Black-Scholes formula to account for dividends.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Adjusts asset price and interest rate in the model based on dividend yield.

methods_unique.name: Black-Scholes Model Adjustment
methods_unique.description: Adjusting Black-Scholes model for early exercise.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value option at each potential exercise date, considering dividends and interest rates.

methods_unique.name: Pseudo-American Valuation
methods_unique.description: Uses Black-Scholes model to value options on ex-dividend days and chooses maximum call values.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Step 1: Define dividend payments. Step 2: Value call option using dividend-adjusted stock price for each ex-dividend day. Step 3: Choose the highest call value.

methods_unique.name: Modified Binomial Model
methods_unique.description: Estimates up and down movements of asset prices based on variance to consider early exercise.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Uses variance in ln(stock prices) to estimate movements, adjusting for riskless rate and dividends.

methods_unique.name: Warrant Valuation Iteration
methods_unique.description: Iterate warrant value estimation until convergence.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Start with an assumed warrant value, estimate dilution-adjusted S, then reestimate warrant’s value using this new S.

methods_unique.name: Black-Scholes valuation
methods_unique.description: Calculates the theoretical price of European put and call options.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: C = SN(d1) - Xe^{-rT}N(d2)

methods_unique.name: Put-Call Parity
methods_unique.description: Relationship between put and call options with same strike price and expiration date.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: P = C - S + Ke^(-rt)

methods_unique.name: Jump Diffusion Model
methods_unique.description: An extension to the Black-Scholes model that incorporates jumps in stock prices, allowing for more realistic modeling of sudden price changes.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of an option depends on five variables from Black-Scholes and parameters of jump process (λ, k)

methods_unique.name: Capped Call Option Valuation
methods_unique.description: Valuing a capped call option involves calculating the difference between the value of an unrestricted call and one with a cap.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Value of Capped Call = Value of Unrestricted Call - Value of Call with Cap

methods_unique.name: Geske's analytical formulation
methods_unique.description: Valuing compound options using a bivariate normal distribution.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Replace the standard normal distribution with a bivariate normal distribution in option valuation.

methods_unique.name: Efficiency Testing
methods_unique.description: Testing investment schemes to prove or disprove market efficiency.

methods_unique.name: Random Diversification
methods_unique.description: Diversifying across stocks or indexing to the market.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Minimize information and execution costs.

methods_unique.name: Minimal Trading Strategy
methods_unique.description: Creating a portfolio and not trading unless cash was needed.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Superior to frequent trading strategies in efficient markets.

methods_unique.name: Proposition 1
methods_unique.description: The probability of finding inefficiencies in an asset market decreases as the ease of trading on the asset increases.

methods_unique.name: Proposition 2
methods_unique.description: The probability of finding an inefficiency in an asset market increases as the transactions and information cost of exploiting the inefficiency increases.

methods_unique.name: Proposition 3
methods_unique.description: The speed with which an inefficiency is resolved will be directly related to how easily the scheme to exploit the inefficiency can be replicated by other investors.

methods_unique.name: Event Study
methods_unique.description: Examines market reactions to specific information events.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Identify event, collect returns around the event date, determine return interval and window size based on precision of event date.

methods_unique.name: Event Study Methodology
methods_unique.description: Calculates abnormal returns around an event date.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: ERjt = Rjt - E(Rjt), t-statistic = average excess return / standard error

methods_unique.name: t statistic estimation
methods_unique.description: Estimate whether event affects stock returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Divide average excess return by standard error to get t statistic.

methods_unique.name: Portfolio Study
methods_unique.description: A method to test investment strategies by creating portfolios of firms with specific characteristics and examining their returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula:
1. Define the variable for classification based on the investment strategy.
2. Collect data at the start of the testing period and classify firms into portfolios.
3. Calculate portfolio returns over the testing period, either equally weighted or value-weighted.
4. Estimate beta or betas for each portfolio.

methods_unique.name: Portfolio Beta Estimation
methods_unique.description: Estimate portfolio beta by averaging individual stock betas or regressing portfolio returns against market returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Beta = Covariance(portfolio returns, market returns) / Variance(market returns)

methods_unique.name: Avoiding Anecdotal Evidence
methods_unique.description: Do not use anecdotal evidence to support or reject an investment strategy due to its inherent bias and noise in stock prices.

methods_unique.name: Out-of-Sample Testing
methods_unique.description: Always test an investment strategy on a different time period or universe than the one from which it was derived to avoid overfitting.

methods_unique.name: Controlling for Market Performance
methods_unique.description: Adjusting investment scheme results to account for overall market performance.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Compare returns against a benchmark index.

methods_unique.name: Risk Adjustment
methods_unique.description: Evaluating investment schemes based on risk-adjusted returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Calculate Sharpe Ratio or Information Ratio.

methods_unique.name: Survival bias
methods_unique.description: Bias in research due to elimination of failed firms.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: None

methods_unique.name: Transaction cost consideration
methods_unique.description: Adjusting returns for transaction costs.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: None

methods_unique.name: Transaction Cost Analysis
methods_unique.description: Evaluating the impact of transaction costs on investment strategy returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Excess Returns - Transaction Costs >= Required Return

methods_unique.name: Serial Correlation
methods_unique.description: Measures correlation between price changes in consecutive time periods.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: A positive serial correlation suggests momentum, while a negative one indicates reversals. Strategies can be devised based on these correlations to potentially earn excess returns.

methods_unique.name: Filter Rule
methods_unique.description: Buys an investment if the price rises X percent from a previous low and holds until the price drops X percent from a previous high.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: X percent change triggers trade, where X varies by rule.

methods_unique.name: 0.5 percent rule
methods_unique.description: A stock is bought when it rises 0.5% from a previous low and sold when it falls 0.5% from a prior high.

methods_unique.name: Price Momentum
methods_unique.description: Stocks that have gone up in the last few weeks or months tend to continue to outperform, and stocks that have gone down tend to underperform.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Jegadeesh and Titman (1993, 2001) present evidence of price momentum over time periods of up to eight months.

methods_unique.name: Price Reversal Strategy
methods_unique.description: Construct portfolios based on past performance to predict future returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Create a 'winner' portfolio of stocks that have performed well in the prior year and a 'loser' portfolio of stocks that have underperformed, then examine their relative performance over the following 60 months.

methods_unique.name: loser portfolio analysis
methods_unique.description: Analyzing portfolios of underperforming stocks.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Consider the impact of low-priced stocks and transaction costs on returns.

methods_unique.name: Speculative Bubble Formation
methods_unique.description: Formation of speculative bubbles due to mispricing and uncertainty.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Not applicable

methods_unique.name: Earnings Report Analysis
methods_unique.description: Measuring market reaction to earnings surprises.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Market reacts instantaneously to surprising information in earnings reports, with positive surprises leading to price increases and negative surprises to declines.

methods_unique.name: Earnings Report Timing Analysis
methods_unique.description: Analyzing the day of the week for earnings reports to predict market reactions.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Negative information is more likely in Friday announcements compared to other days.

methods_unique.name: Market Reaction Analysis
methods_unique.description: Analyzing abnormal returns to investment announcements.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Calculate cumulative abnormal returns (CAR) around announcement dates.

methods_unique.name: Size Effect
methods_unique.description: Investing in smaller firms can yield higher returns.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Risk-adjusted returns for small cap stocks are generally higher than larger firms.

methods_unique.name: Small Firm Premium
methods_unique.description: Additional risk and return associated with small stocks.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Estimate the premium by comparing returns of small vs large firms over a period.

methods_unique.name: Price-Earnings Ratio Screening
methods_unique.description: Identifying undervalued stocks based on low PE ratios.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Screen for stocks in the lowest PE ratio class to find potential excess return opportunities.

methods_unique.name: Low PE Ratio Strategy
methods_unique.description: Investing in stocks with low price-earnings ratios.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Select stocks from the bottom quintile of PE ratios.

methods_unique.name: Price–Book Value Ratio Strategy
methods_unique.description: Investing in stocks with low price-to-book value ratios.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Choose firms with high book-value to market-value ratios.

methods_unique.name: Price-Book Value Ratio Analysis
methods_unique.description: Evaluating stocks based on their price-to-book value ratio.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Calculate the return differential of low price–book value portfolios against market indices.

methods_unique.name: January Effect Analysis
methods_unique.description: Analyzing stock returns in January by size and risk class.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Returns in January for small firms are significantly higher than other months, with half of the premium earned in the first two weeks.

methods_unique.name: January Effect
methods_unique.description: Institutional buying/selling patterns around year-end affect stock prices.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Prices drop before the turn of the year and rise after due to institutional behavior.

methods_unique.name: Weekend Effect
methods_unique.description: Returns on Mondays differ significantly from other days of the week over long periods.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Average daily returns show a pattern with higher returns on Mondays compared to other weekdays.

methods_unique.name: Weekend Effect Analysis
methods_unique.description: Analyzing returns on Mondays and over weekends.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Calculate average daily returns for each day of the week, focusing on Monday and weekend periods.

methods_unique.name: Insider Trading Analysis
methods_unique.description: Analyzing insider trading patterns to predict stock performance.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Compare stocks where insiders have bought more than sold ('buy group') versus those where sells exceed buys ('sell group').

methods_unique.name: Analyst Recommendations Impact
methods_unique.description: Analyzing the impact of buy and sell recommendations on stock prices.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Buy recommendations lead to a 3% price increase, while sell recommendations cause a 4% drop initially. Sell recommendations also result in an additional 5% decline over six months.

methods_unique.name: Jensen's Study
methods_unique.description: Analyzes mutual fund performance relative to market.
methods_unique.rules_or_formula: Excess returns on mutual funds were negative between 1955 and 1964.

methods_unique.name: quartile classification
methods_unique.description: Classifies money managers into quartiles based on performance.
